infobox.key: • Declaration of the Ukrainian Socialist Soviet Republic
• [Admitted to USSR](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Creation_of_the_USSR "Treaty on the Creation of the USSR")

• [Annexation territories](/wiki/Soviet_annexation_of_Western_Ukraine,_1939%E2%80%931940 "Soviet annexation of Western Ukraine, 1939–1940") from [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic")

• [Annexation territories](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina") from [Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania")
• [Received Crimea](/wiki/1954_transfer_of_Crimea "1954 transfer of Crimea") from [Russian SFSR](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic")

• [Priority of Ukrainian laws declared](/wiki/Declaration_of_State_Sovereignty_of_Ukraine "Declaration of State Sovereignty of Ukraine"), partial abolition of the [Soviet form of government](/wiki/Soviet_republic_(system_of_government) "Soviet republic (system of government)")
• [Declaration of independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Ukraine "Declaration of Independence of Ukraine"), Ukrainian SSR renamed to Ukraine
• [Dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") (Ukraine's independence formally recognized)
• Modern [Ukrainian constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine") replaces Soviet constitution

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Ukrainian SSR](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_SSR&redirect=no "Ukrainian SSR"))

Soviet republic from 1919 to 1991

"Communist Ukraine" redirects here. Not to be confused with [Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic_of_Soviets "Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets") or [Ukrainian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Republic"). For the anarcho-communist society in Ukraine, see [Makhnovshchina](/wiki/Makhnovshchina "Makhnovshchina").

The **Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic**,[[b]](#cite_note-15) abbreviated as the **Ukrainian SSR**, **UkrSSR**, **UkSSR** and also known as **Soviet Ukraine** or just **Ukraine**,[[12]](#cite_note-16)[[13]](#cite_note-17)[[14]](#cite_note-18) was one of the [constituent republics](/wiki/Republics_of_the_Soviet_Union "Republics of the Soviet Union") of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") from 1922 until 1991.[[15]](#cite_note-19) Under the Soviet [one-party model](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state"), the Ukrainian SSR was governed by the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") through its [republican branch](/wiki/Soviet_democracy "Soviet democracy"), the [Communist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)").

The first iterations of the Ukrainian SSR were established during the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution"), particularly after the [Bolshevik Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution"). The outbreak of the [Ukrainian–Soviet War](/wiki/Ukrainian%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Ukrainian–Soviet War") in the former [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") saw the [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") defeat the independent [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic"), during the conflict against which they founded the [Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic_of_Soviets "Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets"), which was governed by the [Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") (RSFSR), in December 1917; it was later succeeded by the [Ukrainian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Republic") in 1918.[[16]](#cite_note-20) Simultaneously with the [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"), the [Ukrainian War of Independence](/wiki/Ukrainian_War_of_Independence "Ukrainian War of Independence") was being fought among the different Ukrainian republics founded by [Ukrainian nationalists](/wiki/Ukrainian_nationalism "Ukrainian nationalism"), [Ukrainian anarchists](/wiki/Anarchism_in_Ukraine "Anarchism in Ukraine"), and Ukrainian separatists – primarily against Soviet Russia and the Ukrainian SSR, with either help or opposition from neighbouring states.[[17]](#cite_note-21) In 1922, it was one of four Soviet republics (with the Russian SFSR, the Byelorussian SSR, and the [Transcaucasian SFSR](/wiki/Transcaucasian_Socialist_Federative_Soviet_Republic "Transcaucasian Socialist Federative Soviet Republic")) that signed the [Treaty on the Creation of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Creation_of_the_Union_of_Soviet_Socialist_Republics "Treaty on the Creation of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics"). As a Soviet [quasi-state](/wiki/Quasi-state "Quasi-state"), the Ukrainian SSR became a [founding member of the United Nations](/wiki/Founding_member_of_the_United_Nations "Founding member of the United Nations") in 1945[[18]](#cite_note-22) alongside the [Byelorussian SSR](/wiki/Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic"), in spite of the fact that they were also legally represented by the Soviet Union in foreign affairs. Upon the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") in 1991, the Ukrainian SSR emerged as the present-day independent state of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), although the modified [Soviet-era constitution](/wiki/1978_Constitution_of_the_Ukrainian_SSR "1978 Constitution of the Ukrainian SSR") remained in use until the adoption of the modern [Ukrainian constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine") in June 1996.[[19]](#cite_note-Larousse-23)

The republic's borders changed many times, with a general trend toward acquiring lands with ethnic Ukrainian population majority, and losing lands with other ethnic majorities. A significant portion of what is now [western Ukraine](/wiki/Western_Ukraine "Western Ukraine") was gained via the Soviet-German [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"), with the annexation of [Eastern Galicia and Volhynia](/wiki/Soviet_annexation_of_Eastern_Galicia_and_Volhynia "Soviet annexation of Eastern Galicia and Volhynia") in 1939, significant portions of Romania in 1940, and [Carpathian Ruthenia](/wiki/Carpathian_Ruthenia "Carpathian Ruthenia") in [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") in 1945. From the 1919 establishment of the Ukrainian SSR until 1934, the city of [Kharkov](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") served as its capital; however, the republic's seat of government was subsequently relocated in 1934 to the city of [Kiev](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), the historic Ukrainian capital, and remained at Kiev for the remainder of its existence.

Geographically, the Ukrainian SSR was situated in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), to the north of the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"), and was bordered by the Soviet republics of [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Moldavian Soviet Socialist Republic") (since 1940), Byelorussia, and Russia, and the countries of Romania, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, and Poland. The republic's border with Czechoslovakia formed the Soviet Union's westernmost border point. According to the [1989 Soviet census](/wiki/Soviet_Census_(1989) "Soviet Census (1989)"), the republic of Ukraine had a population of 51,706,746 (second after Russia).[[20]](#cite_note-Starostenko1998-24)[[21]](#cite_note-worldbank1-25)

See also: [Name of Ukraine](/wiki/Name_of_Ukraine "Name of Ukraine")

The original names of the republic in 1919 were both **Ukraine**[[22]](#cite_note-26)[[23]](#cite_note-27)[[24]](#cite_note-28) and **Ukrainian Socialist Soviet Republic** ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Українська Соціалістична Радянська Республіка, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian"): *Ukrainska Sotsialistychna Radianska Respublika*, abbreviated УСРР, *USRR*). After the ratification of the [1936 Soviet Constitution](/wiki/1936_Soviet_Constitution "1936 Soviet Constitution"), full official names of all Soviet republics were changed, transposing the second (*socialist*) and third (*sovietskaya* in Russian or *radianska* in Ukrainian) words. In accordance, on 5 December 1936, the 8th Extraordinary Congress Soviets in Soviet Union changed the name of the republic to the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic, which was ratified by the 14th Extraordinary Congress of Soviets in Ukrainian SSR on 31 January 1937.[[25]](#cite_note-knowbysight-29) This remained the name of the republic until the [1991 declaration of independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Ukraine "Declaration of Independence of Ukraine") when the country became simply Ukraine.

### Establishment: 1917–1922

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Establishment: 1917–1922")]

Further information: [Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)")

#### Aftermath of the revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Aftermath of the revolution")]

Further information: [Ukraine after the Russian Revolution](/wiki/Ukraine_after_the_Russian_Revolution "Ukraine after the Russian Revolution")

After the [Russian Revolution of 1917](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution"), several factions sought to create an independent Ukrainian state, alternately cooperating and struggling against each other. Numerous more or less socialist-oriented factions participated in the formation of the [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic") among which were Bolsheviks, [Mensheviks](/wiki/Mensheviks "Mensheviks"), [Socialists-Revolutionaries](/wiki/Socialist_Revolutionary_Party "Socialist Revolutionary Party") and many others. The most popular faction was initially the local [Socialist Revolutionary Party](/wiki/Ukrainian_Socialist-Revolutionary_Party "Ukrainian Socialist-Revolutionary Party") that composed the local government together with Federalists and Mensheviks.

Following the abdication of Tsar [Nicholas II](/wiki/Nicholas_II_of_Russia "Nicholas II of Russia") during the [February Revolution](/wiki/February_Revolution "February Revolution") of 1917 in [Petrograd](/wiki/Petrograd "Petrograd"), many people in Ukraine wished to establish an autonomous Ukrainian Republic. During a period of the [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War") from 1917 to 1923, many factions claiming themselves governments of the newly born republic were formed, each with supporters and opponents. The two most prominent of them were an independent government in [Kiev](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") called the [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic") (UNR) and a [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic")-aligned government in [Kharkov](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") called the [Ukrainian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Republic") (USR). The Kiev-based UNR was internationally recognized and supported by the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers") following the [Treaty of Brest-Litovsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk_(Ukraine%E2%80%93Central_Powers) "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (Ukraine–Central Powers)"),[[26]](#cite_note-30) whereas the Kharkov-based USR was solely supported by the Soviet Russian forces, while neither the UNR nor the USR were explicitly supported by the [White Russian](/wiki/White_movement "White movement") forces that remained, although there were attempts to establish cooperation during the closing stages of the war with the former.[[27]](#cite_note-31)

#### Early Soviet governments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early Soviet governments")]

See also: [Ukrainian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Republic") and [Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic_of_Soviets "Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets")

Immediately after the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") in [Petrograd](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), Bolsheviks instigated the [Kiev Bolshevik Uprising](/wiki/Kiev_Bolshevik_Uprising "Kiev Bolshevik Uprising") to support the revolution and secure Kiev. Due to a lack of adequate support from the local population and governing anti-communist [Central Rada](/wiki/Central_Rada "Central Rada"), however, the Kiev Bolshevik group split. Most moved to [Kharkov](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") and received the support of the eastern Ukrainian cities and industrial centers. Later, this move was regarded as a mistake by some of the [People's Commissars](/wiki/People%27s_Commissariat "People's Commissariat") ([Yevgenia Bosch](/wiki/Yevgenia_Bosch "Yevgenia Bosch")). They issued an ultimatum to the Central Rada on 17 December to recognise the [Soviet government](/wiki/Council_of_People%27s_Commissars "Council of People's Commissars") of which the Rada was very critical. The Bolsheviks convened a separate congress and declared the first Soviet Republic of Ukraine on 24 December 1917 claiming the [Central Rada](/wiki/Central_Council_of_Ukraine "Central Council of Ukraine") and its supporters outlaws that need to be eradicated. The conflict between the two competing governments, known as the [Ukrainian–Soviet War](/wiki/Ukrainian%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Ukrainian–Soviet War"), was part of the ongoing [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"), as well as the [Ukrainian War of Independence](/wiki/Ukrainian_War_of_Independence "Ukrainian War of Independence").

The government of the Ukrainian Soviet Republic was founded on 24–25 December 1917. In its publications, it named itself either the Republic of Soviets of Workers', Soldiers' and Peasants' Deputies[[28]](#cite_note-32) or the [Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic_of_Soviets "Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets").[[25]](#cite_note-knowbysight-29) The 1917 republic was only recognised by another non-recognised country, the Russian Socialist Federative Soviet Republic. Warfare ensued against the Ukrainian People's Republic for the installation of the Soviet regime in the country, and with the direct support from [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") the Ukrainian National forces were practically overrun. The government of Ukraine appealed to foreign capitals, finding support in the face of the Central Powers as the others refused to recognise it. With the signing of the [Brest-Litovsk Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk") by Russia, the Russian SFSR yielded all the captured Ukrainian territory as the Bolsheviks were forced out of Ukraine, and the Ukrainian People's Republic of Soviets was eventually dissolved.[[29]](#cite_note-33)

In July 1918, the former members of the government formed the [Communist Party (Bolsheviks) of Ukraine](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)"), the constituent assembly of which took place in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"). With the defeat of the Central Powers in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") resumed its hostilities towards the Ukrainian People's Republic fighting for Ukrainian independence and organised another Soviet Ukrainian government. The [Provisional Workers' and Peasants' Government of Ukraine](/wiki/Provisional_Workers%27_and_Peasants%27_Government_of_Ukraine "Provisional Workers' and Peasants' Government of Ukraine") was created on November 28, 1918, in [Kursk](/wiki/Kursk "Kursk"), with its initial seat being located in the city of [Sudzha](/wiki/Sudzha,_Kursk_Oblast "Sudzha, Kursk Oblast"). On 10 March 1919, the [Third All-Ukrainian Congress of Soviets](/wiki/Third_All-Ukrainian_Congress_of_Soviets "Third All-Ukrainian Congress of Soviets") ratified the [Constitution of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Constitutions_of_the_Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic#Constitution_of_1919 "Constitutions of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic") in Kharkiv.[[30]](#cite_note-34) A group of three thousand workers were dispatched from Russia to take grain from local farms to feed Russian cities and were met with resistance. The Ukrainian language was also censured from administrative and educational use. Eventually fighting both White forces in the east and Ukrainian forces in the west, [Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") ordered the liquidation of the second Soviet Ukrainian government in August 1919.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESubtelny2000365-35)

#### Incorporation into the Soviet Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Incorporation into the Soviet Union")]

Formally a sovereign republic until 1922, in view of some historians Soviet Ukraine de-facto came under control of Soviet Russia already in the course of the Civil War. In mid-1919, all republican organs responsible for the management of defence, economy, finance, transport and communications were subordinated to Russia's [people's commissariats](/wiki/People%27s_commissariat "People's commissariat").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201365-66_(volume_2)-36) After the creation of the [Communist Party (Bolshevik) of Ukraine](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)") in Moscow, a third Ukrainian Soviet government was formed on 21 December 1919 that initiated new hostilities against Ukrainian nationalists as they lost their military support from the defeated Central Powers. Eventually, the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") ended up controlling much of the Ukrainian territory after the Polish-Soviet [Peace of Riga](/wiki/Peace_of_Riga "Peace of Riga"). The war ended with the territory of pro-independence Ukrainian People's Republic being annexed into a new Ukrainian Socialist Soviet Republic, and western Ukraine being annexed into the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic").

In 1920–1921 Russia, Ukraine and several other Soviet republics officially established [federative](/wiki/Federation "Federation") ties. In 1921 discussions emerged between two groups of the Bolshevik Party in relation to the nature of relations between Russia and other republics. One fraction, represented by [Stalin](/wiki/Stalin "Stalin"), considered it necessary to incorporate the latter as federal subjects of Russia, meanwhile their opponents, led by [Volodymyr Zatonsky](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zatonsky "Volodymyr Zatonsky"), opposed Russian dominance and supported an equal federation. Meanwhile central Soviet ministries made attempts to establish direct control over enterprises in Ukraine, without consulting the republican government in Kharkiv, which led to protests from local authorities. Following Lenin's sickness, in 1922 Stalin proposed a project of the republics' incorporation into Soviet Russia on the rights of autonomies. However, Lenin opposed that idea, and promoted formal equality of republics including Russia, with simultaneous preservation of Moscow's central control. As a result, Soviet Russia's institutions attained the status of all-Union organs, achieving a higher status than organs of other republics.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201367-79_(volume_2)-37)

On 30 December 1922, along with the [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic"), [Byelorussian](/wiki/Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic") and [Transcaucasian](/wiki/Transcaucasian_Socialist_Federative_Soviet_Republic "Transcaucasian Socialist Federative Soviet Republic") republics, the Ukrainian SSR became one of the founding members of the [Union of Soviet Socialist Republics](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (USSR).[[34]](#cite_note-38) The [1924 Constitution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/1924_Constitution_of_the_Soviet_Union "1924 Constitution of the Soviet Union") formalized this act and declared the state to be based on the principles of [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201380_(volume_2)-39)

### Interwar years: 1922–1939

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Interwar years: 1922–1939")]

#### New Economic Policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: New Economic Policy")]

The policies of [War Communism](/wiki/War_Communism "War Communism") led to the devastation of Ukraine's economy and decline of [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), provoking numerous peasant uprisings against Bolshevik authorities, which had to be suppressed with the use of troops of the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army"). The collapse in the agrarian sphere produced a detrimental effect on the whole economy, and by early 1921 only 4,060 of more than 10,000 industrial enterprises in Ukraine continued their operations. This, in its turn, contributed to the collapse of transport system, with railways coming to a standstill. Even personnel of the Red Army stationed in Ukraine suffered from famine and lack of goods. In these circumstances, central authorities of the [Bolshevik Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") were forced to change their approach to the economy.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201327-36_(volume_2)-40)

The discussions on the introduction of [New Economic Policy](/wiki/New_Economic_Policy "New Economic Policy") (NEP), which started in Moscow cabinets during the early months of 1921, were initially met with skepticism by Ukrainian Communist leadership, which saw [prodrazvyorstka](/wiki/Prodrazvyorstka "Prodrazvyorstka") as an essential component in the reconstruction of economy. After the [10th Congress of the Russian Communist Party (Bolsheviks)](/wiki/10th_Congress_of_the_Russian_Communist_Party_(Bolsheviks) "10th Congress of the Russian Communist Party (Bolsheviks)"), in May 1921 a council of Ukrainian party members in Kharkiv supported the transition to NEP despite some local opposition. A big role in that decision was played by massive peasant unrest. NEP didn't produce any significant changes in the Communist doctrine, but served as a tactical step in order to facilitate the exchange of goods between rural areas and the city. The introduction of the new system signified the failure of the Bolsheviks to rebuild agriculture according to Communist principles.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201336-47_(volume_2)-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Children_affected_by_famine_in_Berdyansk%2C_Ukraine_-_1922.jpg/250px-Children_affected_by_famine_in_Berdyansk%2C_Ukraine_-_1922.jpg)](/wiki/File:Children_affected_by_famine_in_Berdyansk,_Ukraine_-_1922.jpg)

Starving children during the 1921-1923 famine in Ukraine

A severe [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") hit [Southern Ukraine](/wiki/Southern_Ukraine "Southern Ukraine") throughout 1921, at the peak of the Soviet government's campaign of food requisitions from peasants. Unrealistic grain production quotas led to the refusal of peasants to give up their grain, as a result of which authorities answered with terror, including taking of hostages and mass shootings. Despite the visible food problems in Ukraine, the Bolsheviks refused to appeal for international help, concentrating their attention on the [Volga](/wiki/Volga "Volga") region. As a result, a [major famine](/wiki/1921-1923_famine_in_Ukraine "1921-1923 famine in Ukraine") engulfed Southern Ukraine and the [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas"), lasting until 1923. The lack of food resulted in a decline of insurgent activities against Soviet authorities, with some historians viewing the requisitions of food by the government as the first instance of terror through hunger in Ukraine.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201355-65_(volume_2)-42)

#### Strengthening of Communist rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Strengthening of Communist rule")]

In order to increase its influence in regions dominated by non-Russian ethnic groups, the Bolshevik Party leadership developed the program of [korenizatsiya](/wiki/Korenizatsiya "Korenizatsiya"), which provided development of local languages and cultures with simultaneous preservation of centralized control.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201381-83_(volume_2)-43) In Ukrainian SSR during the 1920s this process took the form of [Ukrainization](/wiki/Ukrainization_in_the_1920s_and_1930s "Ukrainization in the 1920s and 1930s") and involved promoting the use and the social status of the Ukrainian language and the elevation of ethnic Ukrainians to leadership positions. These policies provided Ukrainians an impression of national sovereignty, without abandoning the Communist Party's dictatorial rule. The "nationalization" of Soviet bureaucracy also contributed to an increased efficiency of management, strengthening the regime.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201385-86_(volume_2)-44) At the same time, Soviet authorities clearly distinguished between "Communist" and "[Petliurite](/wiki/Symon_Petliura "Symon Petliura")" Ukrainization, seeing the latter as a threat to state unity.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201389_(volume_2)-45) The campaign of Ukrainization aimed to prove the genuine character of Soviet rule in Ukraine and dispel the image of the regime as an alien occupying force. Its course was accelerated by power struggles in the [Kremlin](/wiki/Kremlin "Kremlin") following the death of Lenin, which made it necessary for supreme party leadership to attain support of the Ukrainian society.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201391_(volume_2)-46) At the same time, Russian preserved its role as the language of interethnic communication, with Russians remaining de-facto title nation of the Soviet Union.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201394-95_(volume_2)-47) Ukrainization was halted in 1932-1933, when Stalin's regime proclaimed Ukrainian "[bourgeois nationalism](/wiki/Bourgeois_nationalism "Bourgeois nationalism")" to be the chief threat to Soviet unity.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201384_(volume_2)-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/SVU_trial_1930.jpg/250px-SVU_trial_1930.jpg)](/wiki/File:SVU_trial_1930.jpg)

Trial of alleged members of the "Union for the Freedom of Ukraine" in Kharkiv, 1930

A major role in Soviet Ukraine's internal affairs during the 1920s was played by the [Cheka](/wiki/Cheka "Cheka") secret police and its successor [OGPU](/wiki/OGPU "OGPU") (GPU). Directly subordinated to the [Central Committee](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Central Committee of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union"), those organs functioned as a "state within a state" and were tasked with supporting the party's monopoly on all levels of government and economy. To eliminate opposition to the regime, the secret police employed methods of public terror through [show trials](/wiki/Show_trial "Show trial"), which were an annual occurrence in Ukraine during that period. Chekists were independent from local institutions of government and reported directly to Moscow, which allowed the central government to establish stricter control over affairs in the regions. The head of GPU's Ukrainian branch simultaneously served as the chief envoy of Russian GPU in Ukraine. The secret police also had its own military force, and its departments functioned in the Red Army and in transport enterprises. GPU regularly delivered reports to Ukrainian party leadership, informing them about popular attitudes to the Soviet regime and organizing fictitious "anti-Soviet" organizations in order to eliminate political opponents. One of the most prominent of such "organizations" was the "[Union for the Freedom of Ukraine](/wiki/Union_for_the_Freedom_of_Ukraine_trial "Union for the Freedom of Ukraine trial")".[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013120-132_(volume_2)-49)

#### Collectivization and Holodomor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Collectivization and Holodomor")]

In 1929–1930 a new campaign of grain requisition from peasants was initiated as part of the Soviet regime's [collectivization](/wiki/Collectivization "Collectivization") policies. Simultaneously, tens of thousands of Ukrainians were [deported](/wiki/Deportation "Deportation") in course of [dekulakization](/wiki/Dekulakization "Dekulakization"), causing serious damage to the agricultural workforce. The creation of [collective farms](/wiki/Collective_farm "Collective farm") led to the [pauperization](/wiki/Pauperization "Pauperization") of Ukrainian peasantry and led to cases of armed resistance in various parts of the country. In December 1930 [Central Committee of the Communist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Central Committee of the Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)") adopted a decision to introduce additional taxes and grain requisitions for those villagers who refused to collectivize. As a result, over 70% peasant households in Soviet Ukraine had entered collective farms by late 1932.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013305-332_(volume_2)-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Blackboard_-_punishment_of_kolhoz_during_Holodomor_in_Ukraine.png/250px-Blackboard_-_punishment_of_kolhoz_during_Holodomor_in_Ukraine.png)](/wiki/File:Blackboard_-_punishment_of_kolhoz_during_Holodomor_in_Ukraine.png)

Names of "blacklisted" farms published in a local newspaper in [Mykolaiv Oblast](/wiki/Mykolaiv_Oblast "Mykolaiv Oblast"), 1933

The aggressive agricultural policies of Stalin's regime resulted in one of the largest national catastrophes in the modern history for the [Ukrainian nation](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians"). A famine known as the [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor") caused a direct loss of human life estimated between 2.6 million[[47]](#cite_note-Vallin2005-51)[[48]](#cite_note-Vallinbook-52) to 10 million.[[49]](#cite_note-EGCA-53) The famine was exacerbated by the fact, that grain storages in Soviet Ukraine were controlled by the Soviet Union's central authorities.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013351_(volume_2)-54) In addition, transportation of private grain was outlawed, and peasants from famine-affected areas leaving Ukraine to buy goods in other regions of the USSR were turned back by guards.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013356_(volume_2)-55) The [Law of Spikelets](/wiki/Law_of_Spikelets "Law of Spikelets"), adopted by the [Council of People's Commissars](/wiki/Council_of_People%27s_Commissars "Council of People's Commissars") on 7 August 1932, introduced [execution by firing squad](/wiki/Execution_by_firing_squad "Execution by firing squad") for appropriation of collective farms' property, including grain from uncultivated fields.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013371_(volume_2)-56) Along with mass requisitions of grain, the employment of such methods in Ukraine had a political nature, aiming to eliminate all resistance to the central government.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013391-394_(volume_2)-57) Weakened by the campaign of dekulakization and confiscation of weapons by the Cheka, Ukrainian peasants were unable to effectively resist the regime's policies.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013380_(volume_2)-58) The campaign of government terror against rural inhabitants relied on part of the local cadres, which received preferential treatment, including access to food supplies.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013401-402_(volume_2)-59) The practice of [blacklisting](/wiki/Blacklisting_(Soviet_policy) "Blacklisting (Soviet policy)") resulted in a [blockade](/wiki/Blockade "Blockade") of numerous settlements, whose inhabitants were deprived of all food and died of starvation.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013407_(volume_2)-60) According to a telegram sent by Stalin on 1 January 1933 to Ukrainian Communist leadership, all peasants suspected of hiding grain were to have their entire property, including food products, confiscated.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013417-424_(volume_2)-61) Any mention of the famine was banned by Soviet authorities until the 1980s.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013437-439_(volume_2)-62)

Some scholars and the [World Congress of Free Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainian_World_Congress "Ukrainian World Congress") assert that the famine was [an act of genocide](/wiki/Holodomor_genocide_question "Holodomor genocide question").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [International Commission of Inquiry Into the 1932–1933 Famine in Ukraine](/wiki/International_Commission_of_Inquiry_Into_the_1932%E2%80%931933_Famine_in_Ukraine "International Commission of Inquiry Into the 1932–1933 Famine in Ukraine") found no evidence that the famine was part of a preconceived plan to starve Ukrainians, and concluded in 1990 that the famine was caused by a combination of factors, including Soviet policies of compulsory grain requisitions, forced [collectivization](/wiki/Collective_farming "Collective farming"), [dekulakization](/wiki/Dekulakization "Dekulakization"), and [Russification](/wiki/Russification "Russification").[[59]](#cite_note-Hobbins2001-63) The General Assembly of the UN has stopped shy of recognizing the Holodomor as genocide, calling it a "great tragedy" as a [compromise between tense positions](/wiki/Holodomor_genocide_question "Holodomor genocide question") of United Kingdom, United States, Russia, and Ukraine on the matter, while some nations went on to [individually](/wiki/Holodomor_in_modern_politics "Holodomor in modern politics") categorize it as genocide, including France, Germany, and the United States after the 2022 [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine").

#### Stalinist terror

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Stalinist terror")]

Further information: [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge") and [Executed Renaissance](/wiki/Executed_Renaissance "Executed Renaissance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Vinnycia16.jpg/250px-Vinnycia16.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vinnycia16.jpg)

Bodies of the victims of Soviet terror in Vinnytsia after their exhumation in 1943

Beginning from the late 1920s, Stalin's dictatorial regime introduced a system of total and permanent [terror](/wiki/Terror "Terror") against perceived "remnants of ruling classes", "[counterrevolutionaries](/wiki/Counterrevolution "Counterrevolution")", "[enemies of the people](/wiki/Enemies_of_the_people "Enemies of the people")", "saboteurs", spies and even party members. Even supporters of the regime were persecuted for mere criticism of some of its practices. In order to physically liquidate potential dissenters, GPU and its successor [NKVD](/wiki/NKVD "NKVD") was involved in creation of fictional "counterrevolutionary organizations", with people accused of participating in them being put on trial and executed.[[60]](#cite_note-ency-64)

Among the first groups to become victims of Stalinist terror were [National Communists](/wiki/National_Communism "National Communism"), followed by technical specialists and engineers ([Shakhty Trial](/wiki/Shakhty_Trial "Shakhty Trial")) and members of [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia") ("Union for the Freedom of Ukraine"). As a result of government persecution, several prominent Ukrainian Communists were driven to [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide"), among them [Mykola Khvylyovy](/wiki/Mykola_Khvylyovy "Mykola Khvylyovy"), [Mykola Skrypnyk](/wiki/Mykola_Skrypnyk "Mykola Skrypnyk") and [Panas Liubchenko](/wiki/Panas_Liubchenko "Panas Liubchenko"). Millions of Ukrainians ended up in [concentration camps](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag"), where they were used as cheap labour force, and hundreds of thousands became victims of mass executions, such as [Vinnytsia massacre](/wiki/Vinnytsia_massacre "Vinnytsia massacre"). Mass terror continued until 1938, reaching [its peak](/wiki/Yezhovshchina "Yezhovshchina") during the tenure of [Nikolay Yezhov](/wiki/Nikolay_Yezhov "Nikolay Yezhov"). In Ukraine its main executors were security officers [Martin Latsis](/wiki/Martin_Latsis "Martin Latsis") and [Vsevolod Balitsky](/wiki/Vsevolod_Balitsky "Vsevolod Balitsky"), as well as party secretaries [Pavel Postyshev](/wiki/Pavel_Postyshev "Pavel Postyshev") and [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev").[[60]](#cite_note-ency-64)

### World War II: 1939–1945

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: World War II: 1939–1945")]

Main article: [Modern history of Ukraine § World War II](/wiki/Modern_history_of_Ukraine#World_War_II "Modern history of Ukraine")

#### Annexation of Western Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Annexation of Western Ukraine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Lviv_1939_Sov_Cavalry.jpg/250px-Lviv_1939_Sov_Cavalry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lviv_1939_Sov_Cavalry.jpg)

Soviet cavalry parade in Lviv following the city's surrender to the Red Army on 28 September 1939

In September 1939, following the signing of [Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov-Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact"), the [Soviet Union invaded Poland](/wiki/Soviet_invasion_of_Poland "Soviet invasion of Poland"). Official Soviet propaganda depicted the invasion as a "campaign of liberation" aimed to "free" ethnic [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") and [Belarusians](/wiki/Belarusians "Belarusians") from Polish rule. According to the [Treaty on Friendship and Borders](/wiki/German%E2%80%93Soviet_Boundary_and_Friendship_Treaty "German–Soviet Boundary and Friendship Treaty") from 28 September 1939, Soviet Union and [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") partitioned the Polish state, as a result of which approximately 200,000 sq km of territory with a population of 12 million people was incorporated into the USSR. Following the campaign against Poland, as well as the subsequent [Soviet annexation](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina") of [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") and [Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Northern_Bukovina "Northern Bukovina") from Romania in 1940, newly incorporated territories were divided into [oblasts](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast"), of which [Volyn](/wiki/Volyn_Oblast "Volyn Oblast"), [Drohobych](/wiki/Drohobych_Oblast "Drohobych Oblast"), [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv_Oblast "Lviv Oblast"), [Rivne](/wiki/Rivne_Oblast "Rivne Oblast"), [Stanislaviv](/wiki/Ivano-Frankivsk_Oblast "Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast"), [Ternopil](/wiki/Ternopil_Oblast "Ternopil Oblast"), [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") and [Izmail](/wiki/Izmail_Oblast "Izmail Oblast") oblasts were attached to the Ukrainian SSR. As a result, the republic increased its territory to 560,000 sq km, with its population reaching 41,657,000 inhabitants. Despite government propaganda, the attachment of Western Ukraine to the Soviet Union was seen by many Ukrainians not as a unification of their country, but as an illegitimate [partition of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"). The newly annexed territories underwent rapid Sovietization, with landowners' holdings being divided between peasants. Between February 1940 and June 1941 320,000 people were [forcibly deported](/wiki/Population_transfer_in_the_Soviet_Union "Population transfer in the Soviet Union") from [Western Ukraine](/wiki/Western_Ukraine "Western Ukraine") and [Western Belarus](/wiki/Western_Belarus "Western Belarus"). Polish [prisoners of war](/wiki/Prisoners_of_war "Prisoners of war") were interned in special camps, and more than 14,000 of them were [extrajudicially executed](/wiki/Katyn_massacre "Katyn massacre") in [Katyn](/wiki/Katyn_(rural_locality) "Katyn (rural locality)"), [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") and [Tver](/wiki/Tver "Tver"). The repressions by Stalinist regime had a preventive character, with all people potentially able to resist the regime being eliminated.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201323-35_(volume_3)-65)

#### German–Soviet War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: German–Soviet War")]

Immediately after the start of [German-Soviet War](/wiki/German-Soviet_War "German-Soviet War") in June 1941, Ukraine became a theatre of major battles between [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") and the Red Army. In the [Battle of Brody](/wiki/Battle_of_Brody "Battle of Brody") mechanized units of the Soviet [Southwestern Front](/wiki/Southwestern_Front_(Soviet_Union) "Southwestern Front (Soviet Union)") suffered a crushing defeat from German forces. [Stavka](/wiki/Stavka "Stavka") was reluctant to allow Red Army units an organized retreat, which led to their failure to organize defence on the "[Stalin Line](/wiki/Stalin_Line "Stalin Line")". By 10 July 1941, German forces found themselves on distant approaches to Kyiv. In August, German forces broke the [Southern Front](/wiki/Southern_Front_(Soviet_Union) "Southern Front (Soviet Union)") and [encircled](/wiki/Battle_of_Uman "Battle of Uman") over 100,000 Red Army soldiers at [Uman](/wiki/Uman "Uman"). Advancing from the Moscow direction, tank divisions of [Guderian](/wiki/Heinz_Guderian "Heinz Guderian") and [Kleist](/wiki/Paul_Ludwig_Ewald_von_Kleist "Paul Ludwig Ewald von Kleist") on 15 September closed another ring of encirclement in the vicinity of [Bakhmach](/wiki/Bakhmach "Bakhmach"), [Lokhvytsia](/wiki/Lokhvytsia "Lokhvytsia") and [Lubny](/wiki/Lubny "Lubny"). According to Soviet data, 453,000 Soviet soldiers and officers were captured by Germans in the area. On 18 September Red Army troops [left Kyiv](/wiki/Battle_of_Kiev_(1941) "Battle of Kiev (1941)"), but only a small number of them succeeded to break through German lines and reach Soviet positions. On 30 September Stavka ordered the withdrawal of troops located in [Odesa defensive area](/wiki/Battle_of_Odessa_(1941) "Battle of Odessa (1941)") to [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea"). By October, the Germans had [captured Kharkiv](/wiki/Battle_of_Kharkov_(1941) "Battle of Kharkov (1941)") and [Stalino](/wiki/Donetsk "Donetsk"), advancing to [Mariupol](/wiki/Mariupol "Mariupol"), [Taganrog](/wiki/Taganrog "Taganrog") and [Rostov](/wiki/Rostov "Rostov").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201342-44_(volume_3)-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/%D0%92%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B6%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%B0_1941.jpg/250px-%D0%92%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B6%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%B0_1941.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%92%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B6%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%B0_1941.jpg)

A fire on [Khreshchatyk](/wiki/Khreshchatyk "Khreshchatyk") in Kyiv following the retreat of Soviet troops in September 1941

During the first months of 1942, Soviet forces engaged in fierce battles against Germans in the Donbas. However, a [Red Army counteroffensive](/wiki/Battle_of_Kharkov_(1942) "Battle of Kharkov (1942)") near Kharkiv ended in failure, leading to deaths of 171,000 Soviet troops and capture of another 240,000. In July German forces [captured Sevastopol](/wiki/Siege_of_Sevastopol_(1941-1942) "Siege of Sevastopol (1941-1942)"), eliminating all Soviet regular forces in Crimea. On 27 July 1942 last Soviet troops retreated from [Shchotove](/wiki/Shchotove "Shchotove") in [Luhansk Oblast](/wiki/Luhansk_Oblast "Luhansk Oblast"), signifying the occupation of the whole territory of Soviet Ukraine by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"). In the course of Soviet retreat, starting from June 1941 over 550 industrial enterprises and over 3,5 million workers were evacuated from Ukraine. All objects which couldn't be evacuated were to be destroyed as part of [scorched earth](/wiki/Scorched_earth "Scorched earth") tactics. Among structures demolished during the retreat were the [Dnieper Hydroelectric Station](/wiki/Dnieper_Hydroelectric_Station "Dnieper Hydroelectric Station"), numerous coal mines and many streets in Kyiv, Kharkiv and other cities. The heavy damage to infrastructure produced by such policies exacerbated the state of civilians in occupied territories.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201346-47_(volume_3)-67)

According to various data, between 2,4 and 3,4 million Red Army soldiers were captured by Germans during 1941 only. In Ukraine, most of local captives were released in order not to provoke resistance. However, in many cases captured soldiers would be shot by Germans due to lack of personnel which could be used for guarding them. Mass release of prisoners was stopped in November 1941. Nevertheless, mass surrender of Soviet troops continued well into 1942, demonstrating the lack of support for the Soviet regime, which many recognized to be responsible for deportations, famines and mass repressions. Captured soldiers were forced to live in terrible conditions, with millions of Red Army soldiers perishing in [prisoner camps](/wiki/Stalag "Stalag"). According to statistics, up to two-thirds of Soviet prisoners of war captured in the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)") had died by the end of 1944.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201348-50_(volume_3)-68)

#### Nazi occupation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Nazi occupation")]

Further information: [Reichskommissariat Ukraine](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine "Reichskommissariat Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine_%282%29.png/250px-Reichskommissariat_Ukraine_%282%29.png)](/wiki/File:Reichskommissariat_Ukraine_(2).png)

An administrative map of Reichskommissariat Ukraine as of September 1943

According to the plans of [Hitler](/wiki/Hitler "Hitler") and his closest circle, occupied Ukraine was to become part of the [Greater German Reich](/wiki/Greater_Germanic_Reich "Greater Germanic Reich"). After a treaty signed between Germany and the regime of [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu"), [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa_Oblast "Odesa Oblast"), southern parts of [Vinnytsia](/wiki/Vinnytsia_Oblast "Vinnytsia Oblast") and western part of [Mykolaiv](/wiki/Mykolaiv_Oblast "Mykolaiv Oblast") oblasts were unified into [Transnistria Governorate](/wiki/Transnistria_Governorate "Transnistria Governorate"), and along with Izmail and Chernivtsi oblasts attached to Romania. Lviv, Drohobych, Stanislaviv and Ternopil oblasts were incorporated into the [General Governorate](/wiki/General_Governorate "General Governorate") along with most [occupied Poland](/wiki/Occupied_Poland "Occupied Poland"), meanwhile [Right-bank](/wiki/Right-bank_Ukraine "Right-bank Ukraine") and most of [Left-bank Ukraine](/wiki/Left-bank_Ukraine "Left-bank Ukraine") along with a number of southern areas were put under the civil administration of [Reichskommissariat Ukraine](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine "Reichskommissariat Ukraine"). Crimea and [Eastern Ukraine](/wiki/Eastern_Ukraine "Eastern Ukraine") remained under control of the German [military administration](/wiki/Military_administration "Military administration").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201351-52_(volume_3)-69)

In advance to the invasion, Hitler's regime organized four *[Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen")* tasked with eliminating "ideological and racial enemies" of the Reich. During the years of German occupation, 1,554,000 [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") were murdered in the modern-day borders of Ukraine. Just in two days in late September 1941, members of *Einsatzgruppe C* killed 34,000 Jews in the [Babi Yar](/wiki/Babi_Yar "Babi Yar") ravine near Kyiv. Orders issued by the Wehrmacht supreme command de-facto removed any responsibility for killing of civilians from German soldiers and offciers. In order to support the German war effort, over 2 million of Ukrainians were forcibly mobilized as *[Ostarbeiter](/wiki/Ostarbeiter "Ostarbeiter")* and sent to Germany. Around 450,000 of them died due to lack of food, hard work, poor medical care and [allied bombings](/wiki/Strategic_bombing_during_World_War_II "Strategic bombing during World War II"). Nazi plans for Ukraine included the removal of industrial equipment from the country and its transformation into an agricultural [colony](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"). The German administration refused to disband collective farms, and used their infrastructure for requisition of grain and other products. These policies led to a massive wave of hunger in German-occupied cities, forcing many urban inhabitants to sell their personal belongings in exchange for food. The Nazi [Hunger Plan](/wiki/Hunger_Plan "Hunger Plan") was aimed to eliminate Ukraine's population and replace it with [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") settlers as part of the *[Lebensraum](/wiki/Lebensraum "Lebensraum")* project.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201352-56_(volume_3)-70)

#### Liberation movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Liberation movements")]

Starting from mid-1942, Nazi policies led to a significant growth in the [partisan](/wiki/Partisan_(military) "Partisan (military)") movement in Ukrainian lands. [Soviet partisan](/wiki/Soviet_partisan "Soviet partisan") squads in Ukraine were subordinated to a central headquarters led by [Tymofiy Strokach](/wiki/Tymofiy_Strokach "Tymofiy Strokach"), and by 1942 numbered over 3,000 members. Their biggest detachments were led by [Sydir Kovpak](/wiki/Sydir_Kovpak "Sydir Kovpak"), [Alexander Saburov](/wiki/Alexander_Saburov "Alexander Saburov") and [Oleksiy Fedorov](/wiki/Oleksiy_Fedorov "Oleksiy Fedorov"). Partisans aided the actions of the Red Army by [sabotaging](/wiki/Sabotage "Sabotage") German-controlled railways and bridges, destroying enemy bases and storage facilities. At the same time, members of partisan squads frequently engaged in maraudery against local civilians, causing their hostile attitude to the resistance. After the [Lvov-Sandomierz Offensive](/wiki/Lvov-Sandomierz_Offensive "Lvov-Sandomierz Offensive") in August 1944, most partisans in Ukraine became regular members of the [Soviet Army](/wiki/Soviet_Army "Soviet Army").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201357-61_(volume_3)-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/%D0%90%D0%BA%D1%82_%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%B8.jpg/250px-%D0%90%D0%BA%D1%82_%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%B8.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%90%D0%BA%D1%82_%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%B8.jpg)

Text of the Act of restoration of the Ukrainian state

Simultaneously with Soviet partisans, [Ukrainian nationalists](/wiki/Ukrainian_nationalism "Ukrainian nationalism") also engaged in [guerrilla](/wiki/Guerrilla "Guerrilla") activities in Ukraine during the Second World War. Between 1939 and 1941 the [Organization of Ukrainian Nationalists](/wiki/Organization_of_Ukrainian_Nationalists "Organization of Ukrainian Nationalists") (OUN) allied itself with Nazis due to their mutual hostility against Poland and the USSR. In April 1941, the OUN congress in [Krakow](/wiki/Krakow "Krakow") signified a split in the movement, with the establishment of two separate organizations - the [OUN-B](/wiki/Banderivtsi "Banderivtsi") led by [Stepan Bandera](/wiki/Stepan_Bandera "Stepan Bandera") and the [OUN-M](/wiki/OUN-M "OUN-M") headed by [Andriy Melnyk](/wiki/Andriy_Melnyk_(officer) "Andriy Melnyk (officer)"). Both groups created commissions tasked with establishment of institutions for a future sovereign Ukrainian state. OUN members were supported in their anti-Soviet actions by *[Abwehr](/wiki/Abwehr "Abwehr")* leader [Wilhelm Canaris](/wiki/Wilhelm_Canaris "Wilhelm Canaris"), who allowed the creation of two Ukrainian nationalist battallions under [code names](/wiki/Code_name "Code name") [Roland](/wiki/Roland_Battalion "Roland Battalion") and [Nachtigall](/wiki/Nachtigall_Battallion "Nachtigall Battallion"). After the retreat of Soviet troops, the number of Bandera's armed supporters in western regions of Ukraine reached 12,000. On 29 June 1941 OUN members launched an uprising in [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv"), and after the entry of German troops on the next day OUN-B member [Yaroslav Stetsko](/wiki/Yaroslav_Stetsko "Yaroslav Stetsko") proclaimed the [Act of restoration of the Ukrainian state](/wiki/Act_of_restoration_of_the_Ukrainian_state "Act of restoration of the Ukrainian state"). However, already on 15 July German police performed arrests of OUN-B members, with leaders of the organization being sent to [Sachsenhausen concentration camp](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_concentration_camp "Sachsenhausen concentration camp").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201361-65_(volume_3)-72)

Starting from October 1942, Ukrainian nationalists initiated the formation of the [Ukrainian Insurgent Army](/wiki/Ukrainian_Insurgent_Army "Ukrainian Insurgent Army") (UPA), whose units engaged in fighting against both German occupiers and Soviet partisans. In autumn 1943 UPA was formally subordinated to the [Ukrainian Supreme Liberation Council](/wiki/Ukrainian_Supreme_Liberation_Council "Ukrainian Supreme Liberation Council") dominated by members of OUN-B. Supporters of Melnyk refused to enter the organization. Blaming local Poles of collaboration with Nazis, in July 1943 UPA forces under command of [Dmytro Klyachkivsky](/wiki/Dmytro_Klyachkivsky "Dmytro Klyachkivsky") organized a [campaign of ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Volhynian_massacres "Volhynian massacres") against the Polish inhabitants in [Volhynia](/wiki/Volhynia "Volhynia"). In November 1943 Klyachkivsky was removed from his post and replaced by [Roman Shukhevych](/wiki/Roman_Shukhevych "Roman Shukhevych").[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201372-76_(volume_3)-73)

#### Soviet counteroffensive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Soviet counteroffensive")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Dayosh_Kiev.jpg/250px-Dayosh_Kiev.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dayosh_Kiev.jpg)

[Soviet soldiers](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") preparing rafts to cross the [Dnieper](/wiki/Dnieper "Dnieper") during the [Battle of the Dnieper](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Dnieper "Battle of the Dnieper") (1943). The sign in [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") reads: "Let's get Kiev!"

Following the [Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad"), between December 1942 and February 1943 Soviet forces expelled German troops from easternmost parts of Ukraine up to the [Mius](/wiki/Mius "Mius") river. Following the [Battle of Kursk](/wiki/Battle_of_Kursk "Battle of Kursk"), in Autumn 1943 Soviet armies [advanced to the Dnieper](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Dnieper "Battle of the Dnieper"), and on 6 November [entered Kyiv](/wiki/Battle_of_Kiev_(1943) "Battle of Kiev (1943)"). In the course of [Korsun](/wiki/Korsun-Shevchenkovsky_Offensive "Korsun-Shevchenkovsky Offensive") and [Nikopol-Kryvyi Rih](/wiki/Nikopol%E2%80%93Krivoi_Rog_Offensive "Nikopol–Krivoi Rog Offensive") offensives, Soviet Union reestablished control over large parts of Right-bank Ukraine, and in March 1944 forces of the [1st Ukrainian Front](/wiki/1st_Ukrainian_Front "1st Ukrainian Front") took [Vinnytsia](/wiki/Vinnytsia "Vinnytsia"), [Proskuriv](/wiki/Khmelnytskyi "Khmelnytskyi") and [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi"). In April armies of the [3rd Ukrainian Front](/wiki/3rd_Ukrainian_Front "3rd Ukrainian Front") took Odesa, and on 9 May units of the [4th Ukrainian Front](/wiki/4th_Ukrainian_Front "4th Ukrainian Front") entered [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol"), establishing control over Crimea. Following [Operation Bagration](/wiki/Operation_Bagration "Operation Bagration"), Soviet armies advanced through Western Ukraine into Poland, and by 28 October 1944 expelled German troops from [Transcarpathia](/wiki/Transcarpathia "Transcarpathia").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201367-72_(volume_3)-74)

The return of Soviet authorities to Ukraine was accompanied with mass forced [mobilization](/wiki/Mobilization "Mobilization") of locals, aimed to deprive Ukraianian nationalists of their pool for recruitment of new members. In order to combat nationalist guerrilla activities, Soviet [NKVD](/wiki/NKVD "NKVD") organized special groups formed of captured UPA members, which were tasked with infiltrating the organization and eliminating its members. The Soviet regime also practiced [collective punishment](/wiki/Collective_punishment "Collective punishment") against family members of insurgents. According to official data, between 1944 and 1945 over 103,000 rebels were killed and over 110,000 captured. Over 30,000 people were deported from Western Ukraine to remote regions of the USSR for their cooperation with anti-Soviet resistance.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201377-78_(volume_3)-75)

### Post-war years: 1945–1953

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Post-war years: 1945–1953")]

#### Aftermath of war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Aftermath of war")]

While [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") (called the [Great Patriotic War](/wiki/Great_Patriotic_War_(term) "Great Patriotic War (term)") by the Soviet government) did not end before May 1945, the Germans were driven out of Ukraine between February 1943 and October 1944. The first task of the Soviet authorities was to reestablish political control over the republic which had been entirely lost during the war. This was an immense task, considering the widespread human and material losses. During World War II [the Soviet Union lost](/wiki/World_War_II_casualties_of_the_Soviet_Union "World War II casualties of the Soviet Union") about 8.6 million combatants and around 18 million civilians, of these, 6.8 million were Ukrainian civilians and military personnel. Also, an estimated 3.9 million Ukrainians were evacuated to the [Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") during the war, and 2.2 million Ukrainians were sent to forced labour camps by the Germans.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The material devastation was huge; [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")'s orders to create "a zone of annihilation" in 1943, coupled with the Soviet military's scorched-earth policy in 1941, meant Ukraine lay in ruins. These two policies led to the destruction of more than 28,000 villages and 714 cities and towns. 85 percent of Kyiv's city centre was destroyed, as was 70 percent of the city centre of the second-largest city in Ukraine, Kharkiv. Because of this, 19 million people were left homeless after the war.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996684-76) The republic's industrial base, as so much else, was destroyed.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996684–685-77) The Soviet government had managed to evacuate 544 industrial enterprises between July and November 1941, but the rapid German advance led to the destruction or the partial destruction of 16,150 enterprises. 27,910 [collective farms](/wiki/Collective_farming "Collective farming"), 1,300 machine tractor stations and 872 state farms were destroyed by the Germans.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996685-78)

#### Expansion and international recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Expansion and international recognition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Curzon_line_en.svg/250px-Curzon_line_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Curzon_line_en.svg)

The [Curzon Line](/wiki/Curzon_Line "Curzon Line") expanded the territory of the Ukrainian SSR to include western Ukraine, previously controlled by [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/ZakapratskaUkraina1944.png/250px-ZakapratskaUkraina1944.png)](/wiki/File:ZakapratskaUkraina1944.png)

Front page of the *Zakarpattia Ukraine* newspaper (1944) with the manifest of unification with Soviet Ukraine

While the war brought to Ukraine an enormous physical destruction, victory also led to territorial expansion. As a victor, the Soviet Union gained new prestige and more land.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996685-78) During the [Tehran Conference](/wiki/Tehran_Conference "Tehran Conference") in 1943, [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister") [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") negotiated a plan with Stalin, according to which lands of Poland populated by ethnic Ukrianians and Belarusians would be transferred to Soviet Ukraine and Soviet Belarus respectively, in exchange for which Poland would receive [parts of German territory](/wiki/Recovered_Territories "Recovered Territories"). The new Polish-Ukrainian border would generally follow the [Curzon Line](/wiki/Curzon_Line "Curzon Line"). Following the expulsion of Germans, between 1944 and 1946 [Poland](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic") and Soviet Ukraine performed a [population exchange](/wiki/Population_exchange "Population exchange"), in course of which 810,400 Poles were resettled from Ukraine into Poland, and 482,900 Ukrainians had to leave Poland for Ukraine.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201384-90_(volume_3)-79) Further 150,000 of Ukrainians would be deported from Polish territory during the [Operation Vistula](/wiki/Operation_Vistula "Operation Vistula") in 1947. A population exchange was also organized between Ukraine and Czechoslovakia.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013110-111_(volume_3)-80)

On 26 November 1944, a council of people's committees in Transcarpathia adopted a resolution on the creation of a "People's Council" headed by [Czechoslovak Communist](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Czechoslovakia "Communist Party of Czechoslovakia") [Ivan Turyanytsia](/wiki/Ivan_Turyanytsia "Ivan Turyanytsia"), and proclaimed the unification of the region with Soviet Ukraine. In May 1945 [Avhustyn Voloshyn](/wiki/Avhustyn_Voloshyn "Avhustyn Voloshyn"), the former president of [Carpatho-Ukraine](/wiki/Carpatho-Ukraine "Carpatho-Ukraine"), was arrested by Soviet secret police, and on 29 June 1945 [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") officially agreed to transfer [Carpathian Ruthenia](/wiki/Carpathian_Ruthenia "Carpathian Ruthenia") to the Ukrainian SSR.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201390-91_(volume_3)-81) Ukraine was also expanded southwards, near the area [Izmail](/wiki/Izmail "Izmail"), previously part of Romania.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996685-78) As a result, the territory of Ukraine expanded by 167,000 square kilometres (64,500 sq mi) and the republic increased its population by an estimated 11 million.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996688-82)

After World War II, amendments to the Constitution of the Ukrainian SSR were accepted, which allowed it to act as a separate subject of [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law") in some cases and to a certain extent, remaining a part of the Soviet Union at the same time. In particular, these amendments allowed the Ukrainian SSR to become one of the founding members of the United Nations (UN) together with the Soviet Union and the [Byelorussian SSR](/wiki/Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic"). This was part of a deal with the United States to ensure a degree of balance in the [General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly"), which, the USSR opined, was unbalanced in favor of the Western Bloc. In its capacity as a member of the UN, the Ukrainian SSR was [an elected member](/wiki/List_of_members_of_the_United_Nations_Security_Council "List of members of the United Nations Security Council") of the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") in 1948–1949 and 1984–1985.[[79]](#cite_note-83) In 1954 Ukraine also became a member of [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013111_(volume_3)-84)

#### Soviet policies and resistance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Soviet policies and resistance")]

Despite a severe drought in the country, in 1946 Soviet authorities refused to decrease the planned amounts of harvest in Ukraine, and signed agreements on grain exports to Poland, [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgarian_People%27s_Republic "Bulgarian People's Republic"), Romania and Czechoslovakia in order to support the local Comunist movements. As a result, [a new famine](/wiki/Soviet_famine_of_1946-1947_in_Ukraine "Soviet famine of 1946-1947 in Ukraine") started in the Ukrainian province, leading to hundreds of thousands of deaths. Unlike in 1933, relief to the affected areas was provided both by the Soviet authorities and by the [UNRRA](/wiki/UNRRA "UNRRA"). At the same time, in order to raise the productivity of agriculture, the Soviet central government adopted the [Great Plan for the Transformation of Nature](/wiki/Great_Plan_for_the_Transformation_of_Nature "Great Plan for the Transformation of Nature"), introduced community trials for unproductive farmers and attempted to increase the size of collective farms. In 1955 the party leadership adopted a resolution, ordering local authorities to dispatch 30,000 young specialists for work in collective farms and [sovkhozes](/wiki/Sovkhoz "Sovkhoz"). Starting from 1954, hundreds of thousands of agricultural workers were sent to the [Kazakh SSR](/wiki/Kazakh_SSR "Kazakh SSR") as part of [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev")'s [Virgin Lands campaign](/wiki/Virgin_Lands_campaign "Virgin Lands campaign").[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013119-123_(volume_3)-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/%D0%9F%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96_%D0%A3%D0%9F%D0%90_%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D1%85%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B6%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0_%D0%9A%D1%83%D0%B7%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%B0_%C2%AB%D0%9A%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%8F%C2%BB.jpg/250px-%D0%9F%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96_%D0%A3%D0%9F%D0%90_%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D1%85%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B6%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0_%D0%9A%D1%83%D0%B7%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%B0_%C2%AB%D0%9A%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%8F%C2%BB.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9F%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96_%D0%A3%D0%9F%D0%90_%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D1%85%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B6%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0_%D0%9A%D1%83%D0%B7%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%B0_%C2%AB%D0%9A%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%8F%C2%BB.jpg)

A group of UPA rebels during the late 1940s or early 1950s

Despite the Soviet occupation and incorporation of Western Ukraine during WW2, [Anti-Soviet resistance by the Ukrainian Insurgent Army](/wiki/Anti-Soviet_resistance_by_the_Ukrainian_Insurgent_Army "Anti-Soviet resistance by the Ukrainian Insurgent Army") in the region continued for a decade after the war's end. In order to break the insurgency, Soviet authorities sent regular troops and NKVD formations in order to establish a blockade of rebel areas. Nevertheless, throughout 1946 OUN and UPA engaged in 1619 actions against the regime and its forces. By 1949, the base of insurgency had been significantly damaged as a result of special operations, deportations and collectivization, and UPA commander Roman Shukhevych ordered the incorporation of all rebel units into the structure of OUN-B. On 5 March 1950 Shukhevych was killed in battle in the village of Bilohorshcha near Lviv following a search operation led by [Pavel Sudoplatov](/wiki/Pavel_Sudoplatov "Pavel Sudoplatov"). During the next two years, [MGB](/wiki/Ministry_of_State_Security_(Soviet_Union) "Ministry of State Security (Soviet Union)") managed to eliminate several high-ranked rebels by infiltrating the underground organization. In May 1954 [Vasyl Kuk](/wiki/Vasyl_Kuk "Vasyl Kuk"), the last commander of UPA, was captured by Soviet authorities. After that, the nationalist insurgency gradually ceased, although minor operations against the rebels continued until 1960.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013124-127_(volume_3)-86)

### Khrushchev and Brezhnev: 1953–1982

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Khrushchev and Brezhnev: 1953–1982")]

#### Khrushchev Thaw

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Khrushchev Thaw")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/1954._300-%D0%BB%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%B2%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B_%D1%81_%D0%A0%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%B5%D0%B9.jpg/250px-1954._300-%D0%BB%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%B2%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B_%D1%81_%D0%A0%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%B5%D0%B9.jpg)](/wiki/File:1954._300-%D0%BB%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%B2%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B_%D1%81_%D0%A0%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%B5%D0%B9.jpg)

Soviet [postal stamp](/wiki/Postage_stamp "Postage stamp"), 1954, in honour of the 300th anniversary of Ukraine re-unification with Russia (Soviet name for the [Pereiaslav Agreement](/wiki/Pereiaslav_Agreement "Pereiaslav Agreement")), Russian: 300-летие Воссоединения Украины с Россией).

When Stalin died on 5 March 1953, the [collective leadership](/wiki/Collective_leadership "Collective leadership") of Khrushchev, [Georgy Malenkov](/wiki/Georgy_Malenkov "Georgy Malenkov"), [Vyacheslav Molotov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Molotov "Vyacheslav Molotov") and [Lavrentiy Beria](/wiki/Lavrentiy_Beria "Lavrentiy Beria") took power and a period of [de-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization") began.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996701-87) Change came as early as 1953, when officials were allowed to criticise Stalin's policy of [russification](/wiki/Russification "Russification"). The Central Committee of the [Communist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)") (CPU) openly criticised Stalin's russification policies in a meeting in June 1953. On 4 June 1953, [Aleksey Kirichenko](/wiki/Aleksey_Kirichenko "Aleksey Kirichenko") succeeded [Leonid Melnikov](/wiki/Leonid_Melnikov "Leonid Melnikov") as First Secretary of the CPU; this was significant since Kyrychenko was the first ethnic Ukrainian to lead the CPU since the 1920s. The policy of de-Stalinization took two main features, that of centralisation and decentralisation from the centre. In February 1954, the [Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") (RSFSR) [transferred Crimea](/wiki/Transfer_of_Crimea_in_the_Soviet_Union "Transfer of Crimea in the Soviet Union") to Ukraine during the celebrations of the 300th anniversary of Ukraine's reunification with Russia, Soviet term for the [Pereiaslav Agreement](/wiki/Pereiaslav_Agreement "Pereiaslav Agreement") ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Переяславська рада), a treaty which brought Ukraine under Russian rule three centuries before.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996702–703-88) The massive festivities lasted throughout 1954, aiming to prove the old and brotherly love between [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") and [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians"), and depict the Soviet Union as a "family of nations"; it was also a way of legitimising [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism").[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996703-89)

The ["Thaw"](/wiki/Khrushchev_Thaw "Khrushchev Thaw") – the policy of deliberate liberalisation – was characterised by four points: amnesty for some convicted of state crime during the war or the immediate post-war years; amnesties for one-third of those convicted of state crime during Stalin's rule; the establishment of the first Ukrainian mission to the United Nations in 1958; and the steady increase of Ukrainians in the rank of the CPU and government of the Ukrainian SSR. Not only were the majority of CPU Central Committee and Politburo members ethnic Ukrainians, three-quarters of the highest ranking party and state officials were ethnic Ukrainians too. The policy of partial [Ukrainisation](/wiki/Ukrainisation "Ukrainisation") also led to a cultural thaw within Ukraine.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996703-89) Late 1950s saw the emergence of Ukraine's [dissident](/wiki/Soviet_dissidents "Soviet dissidents") movement. In 1958 [Levko Lukianenko](/wiki/Levko_Lukianenko "Levko Lukianenko") established the underground "Ukrainian Workers' and Peasants' Union", whose members were arrested three years later and sentenced to long terms of imprisonment. The process against the organization was publicized in foreign media through the efforts of [Ivan Svitlychnyi](/wiki/Ivan_Svitlychnyi "Ivan Svitlychnyi").[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013196-197_(volume_3)-90)

#### Brezhnev era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Brezhnev era")]

Further information: [Era of Stagnation](/wiki/Era_of_Stagnation "Era of Stagnation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/%D0%A5%D1%80%D1%83%D1%89%D1%91%D0%B2_%D0%B8_%D0%91%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B2.jpg/250px-%D0%A5%D1%80%D1%83%D1%89%D1%91%D0%B2_%D0%B8_%D0%91%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B2.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A5%D1%80%D1%83%D1%89%D1%91%D0%B2_%D0%B8_%D0%91%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B2.jpg)

Two Soviet [general secretaries](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") were either born or raised in Ukraine: [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") and [Leonid Brezhnev](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev") (depicted here together).

In October 1964, Khrushchev was deposed by a joint Central Committee and Politburo plenum and succeeded by another collective leadership, this time led by [Leonid Brezhnev](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev"), born in Ukraine, as First Secretary and [Alexei Kosygin](/wiki/Alexei_Kosygin "Alexei Kosygin") as [Chairman](/wiki/Premier_of_the_Soviet_Union "Premier of the Soviet Union") of the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Soviet_Union "Council of Ministers of the Soviet Union").[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996708-91) Brezhnev's rule would be marked by social and economic stagnation.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996708–709-92) The new regime introduced the policy of *rastsvet*, *sblizhenie* and *sliianie* ("flowering", "drawing together" and "merging"/"fusion"), which was the policy of uniting the different Soviet nationalities into one [Soviet nationality](/wiki/Soviet_people "Soviet people") by merging the best elements of each nationality into the new one. This policy turned out to be, in fact, the reintroduction of the [Russification](/wiki/Russification_of_Ukraine "Russification of Ukraine").[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996709-93) Brezhnev's tenure saw the gradual elimination of previously established distinctions between party oligarchy and the [nomenklatura](/wiki/Nomenklatura "Nomenklatura").[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013231_(volume_3)-94) During its later years, the regime established by Brezhnev and his circle of aging Communist party officials was frequently described as a [gerontocracy](/wiki/Gerontocracy "Gerontocracy").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013235-237_(volume_3)-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%9A%D2%90%D0%91_8-09-1965.JPG/250px-%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%9A%D2%90%D0%91_8-09-1965.JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%9A%D2%90%D0%91_8-09-1965.JPG)

A KGB report from 8 September 1965 concerning the protest organized by Dziuba and Chornovil

Brezhnev's ascent to leadership was widely seen as a sign of restoration of [Stalinist](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") practices in Soviet politics, and produced hostile attitudes among Ukrainian intelligentsia. In order to suppress the opposition, in September 1965 [First Secretary of the Communist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/First_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_Ukraine "First Secretary of the Communist Party of Ukraine") [Petro Shelest](/wiki/Petro_Shelest "Petro Shelest") ordered the [KGB](/wiki/KGB "KGB") to perform [a series of arrests](/wiki/1965-1966_Ukrainian_purge "1965-1966 Ukrainian purge") against prominent dissdents. During the operation, numerous copies of [samizdat](/wiki/Samizdat "Samizdat") literature were confiscated.
