After learning about the arrests, [Ivan Dziuba](/wiki/Ivan_Dziuba "Ivan Dziuba") and [Viacheslav Chornovil](/wiki/Viacheslav_Chornovil "Viacheslav Chornovil") organized Ukraine's first [public protest](/wiki/Public_protest "Public protest") in decades, which took place during the screening of [Sergei Parajanov](/wiki/Sergei_Parajanov "Sergei Parajanov")'s film *[Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors](/wiki/Shadows_of_Forgotten_Ancestors "Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors")* in Kyiv's Ukraina cinema. In 1968 Dziuba's [pamphlet](/wiki/Pamphlet "Pamphlet") *[Internationalism or Russification?](/wiki/Internationalism_or_Russification%3F "Internationalism or Russification?")*, protesting against Russian [chauvinism](/wiki/Chauvinism "Chauvinism"), was published by *[Suchasnist](/wiki/Suchasnist "Suchasnist")* publishing house in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), spreading awareness about Ukrainian intellectual resistance both in Ukraine and abroad. Both Dziuba and Chornovil never hid their dissident views, but instead openly contacted Soviet Ukraine's governing circles, demanding political change. After spending three years in imprisonment for his activities, in 1970 Chornovil started publishing *[The Ukrainian Herald](/wiki/The_Ukrainian_Herald_(1970-1972,_1987-1989) "The Ukrainian Herald (1970-1972, 1987-1989)")*, which chronicled government repressions against Ukrainian activists.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013243-249_(volume_3)-96)

In January 1972 [a new massive campaign](/wiki/1972%E2%80%931973_Ukrainian_purge "1972–1973 Ukrainian purge") against Ukrainian dissidents was started by authorities, producing significant damage to the opposition movement. However, following the [Helsinki Accords](/wiki/Helsinki_Accords "Helsinki Accords") of 1975, a new wave of dissident activities started in form of the [Human rights movement](/wiki/Human_rights_movement_in_the_Soviet_Union "Human rights movement in the Soviet Union"). In November 1976 the [Ukrainian Helsinki Group](/wiki/Ukrainian_Helsinki_Group "Ukrainian Helsinki Group") was established in Kyiv by [Mykola Rudenko](/wiki/Mykola_Rudenko "Mykola Rudenko"), [Oles Berdnyk](/wiki/Oles_Berdnyk "Oles Berdnyk"), [Petro Hryhorenko](/wiki/Petro_Hryhorenko "Petro Hryhorenko"), [Ivan Kandyba](/wiki/Ivan_Kandyba "Ivan Kandyba"), Levko Lukianenko and several other activists. Soviet authorities started an immediate crackdown on the group, and in the following years many of its members were arrested, while others, such as Hryhorenko and [Leonid Plyushch](/wiki/Leonid_Plyushch "Leonid Plyushch"), were forced to emigrate and continued the organization's activities abroad. Several political prisoners, including [Oleksa Tykhy](/wiki/Oleksa_Tykhy "Oleksa Tykhy"), [Yuriy Lytvyn](/wiki/Yuriy_Lytvyn_(dissident) "Yuriy Lytvyn (dissident)") and [Vasyl Stus](/wiki/Vasyl_Stus "Vasyl Stus"), died in imprisonment.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013250-252_(volume_3)-97)

### Gorbachev to independence: 1985–1991

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Gorbachev to independence: 1985–1991")]

#### Liberalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Liberalization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/IAEA_02790014_%285613114630%29.jpg/250px-IAEA_02790014_%285613114630%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:IAEA_02790014_(5613114630).jpg)

Use of water for [decontamination](/wiki/Decontamination "Decontamination") in Kyiv 5 days after the Chernobyl disaster, 1 May 1986

Gorbachev's policies of *[perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika")* and *[glasnost](/wiki/Glasnost "Glasnost")* (English: *restructuring* and *openness*) failed to reach Ukraine as early as other Soviet republics because of the influence of [Volodymyr Shcherbytsky](/wiki/Volodymyr_Shcherbytsky "Volodymyr Shcherbytsky"), a conservative communist appointed by Brezhnev and the First Secretary of the Ukrainian Communist Party.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996717-98) The [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") of 1986, the russification policies, and the apparent social and economic stagnation led many Ukrainians to oppose Soviet rule. Gorbachev's policy of *perestroika* was also never introduced into practice, 95 percent of industry and agriculture was still owned by the Soviet state in 1990. The talk of reform, but the lack of introducing reform into practice, led to confusion which in turn evolved into opposition to the Soviet state itself.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996718–719-99) The policy of *glasnost*, which ended [state censorship](/wiki/Censorship_in_the_Soviet_Union "Censorship in the Soviet Union"), led the [Ukrainian diaspora](/wiki/Ukrainian_diaspora "Ukrainian diaspora") to reconnect with their compatriots in Ukraine, the revitalisation of religious practices by destroying the monopoly of the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") and led to the establishment of several opposition pamphlets, journals and newspapers.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996720–721-100)

Glasnost policies resulted in the publication of previously classified information on Soviet history, including materials on the Holodomor of 1932–1933 and produced a significant change in outlooks of the Ukrainian elite. Among prominent people who became disillusioned with the Soviet regime were Ukrainian Central Committee member [Leonid Kravchuk](/wiki/Leonid_Kravchuk "Leonid Kravchuk") and poet [Dmytro Pavlychko](/wiki/Dmytro_Pavlychko "Dmytro Pavlychko"), both of whom adopted positions critical of Moscow's Communist rule.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013293-294_(volume_3)-101) The issue of Holodomor was first mentioned on the highest level in July 1988 during [Borys Oliynyk](/wiki/Borys_Oliynyk_(poet) "Borys Oliynyk (poet)")'s speech at the 19th all-Union Party Conference. In his interview given in November of the same year, writer [Yuriy Shcherbak](/wiki/Yuriy_Shcherbak "Yuriy Shcherbak") called the famine an element of terror against the Ukrainian national liberation movement. In 1990 the Institute of Party History of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of Ukraine published a collection of documents dedicated to the famine, which was approved by first secretary [Volodymyr Ivashko](/wiki/Vladimir_Ivashko "Vladimir Ivashko"). Simultaneously, publication of previously banned works on Ukrainian history, including [Mykhailo Hrushevsky](/wiki/Mykhailo_Hrushevsky "Mykhailo Hrushevsky")'s *[History of Ukraine-Rus](/wiki/History_of_Ukraine-Rus "History of Ukraine-Rus")*, texts by [Dmytro Yavornytsky](/wiki/Dmytro_Yavornytsky "Dmytro Yavornytsky") and [Mykhailo Drahomanov](/wiki/Mykhailo_Drahomanov "Mykhailo Drahomanov"), memoirs of [Volodymyr Vynnychenko](/wiki/Volodymyr_Vynnychenko "Volodymyr Vynnychenko") and the *[History of Ruthenians](/wiki/History_of_Ruthenians "History of Ruthenians")* was initiated by a Ukrainian party commission.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013296-298_(volume_3)-102)

#### Rise of civil society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Rise of civil society")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/%D0%93%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%81%D1%96%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D1%81%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%BA%D0%B0-1.jpg/250px-%D0%93%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%81%D1%96%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D1%81%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%BA%D0%B0-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%93%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%81%D1%96%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D1%81%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%BA%D0%B0-1.jpg)

Members of the Ukrainian Helsinki Union in 1989 with Chornovil standing 3rd from left

The beginning of Perestroika was signified by the emergence of Soviet Ukraine's first non-government civic organizations. The first of them, the [Ukrainian Culturological Club](/wiki/Ukrainian_Culturological_Club "Ukrainian Culturological Club") and the [Lion's Society](/wiki/Lion%27s_Society "Lion's Society"), were establihsed in 1987 by youth representatives and dissidents. A big role after the Chernobyl Disaster was played by the [ecology movement](/wiki/Environmental_movement "Environmental movement"). In March 1988 former members of the Ukrainian Helsinki Group established the [Ukrainian Helsinki Union](/wiki/Ukrainian_Helsinki_Union "Ukrainian Helsinki Union") under the leadership of Levko Lukianenko, who was released from prison later that year. In 1987 *The Ukrainian Herald* resumed publishing after a 15-year pause, making it the first legal independent political publication in Ukraine.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013306-307_(volume_3)-103) In February 1989 the Shevchenko Ukrainian Language Society was established under the leadership of Dmytro Pavlychko. In October of the same year the Supreme Council of Ukrainian SSR adopted the [Law on Languages](/wiki/Language_policy_in_Ukraine#1989_Law_of_the_Languages "Language policy in Ukraine"), which officially recognized Ukrainian as [state language](/wiki/State_language "State language").[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013309-310_(volume_3)-104)

Starting from 1988, first mass demonstrations were organized by non-governmental organizations in several Ukrainian cities. Their participants voiced opposition to the suppression of Ukrainian language, condemned plans to construct new nuclear power plants and commemorated victims of the Holodomor and Stalinist repression. In summer 1989 [miners' strikes](/wiki/1990s_Donbas_miners%27_strikes "1990s Donbas miners' strikes") engulfed [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas") and the [Lviv-Volyn coal basin](/wiki/Lviv-Volyn_coal_basin "Lviv-Volyn coal basin"), with their participants demanding better working conditions and higher wages.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013310-312_(volume_3)-105) On 8-10 September 1989 the constituent congress of the [People's Movement of Ukraine](/wiki/People%27s_Movement_of_Ukraine "People's Movement of Ukraine") (Rukh) was organized in Kyiv, with [Ivan Drach](/wiki/Ivan_Drach "Ivan Drach") being elected as its leader. The movement's program didn't contain demands for immediate proclamation of Ukraine's independence, but voiced support of reformist forces in the Communist Party. On 21 January 1990 Rukh activists organized a [human chain](/wiki/Human_chain_(politics) "Human chain (politics)") in memory of the 1919 [Unification Act](/wiki/Act_Zluky "Act Zluky") between the [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic") and [West Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "West Ukrainian People's Republic"), during which hundreds of thousands of participants connected Kyiv with [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv") and [Ivano-Frankivsk](/wiki/Ivano-Frankivsk "Ivano-Frankivsk"). By October 1990 the number of Rukh's activists had increased to 633,000, and it enjoyed support from over 5 million people.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013318-320_(volume_3)-106)

#### Economic reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Economic reforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/%D0%A0%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BD%D1%96_%D1%89%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B8_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B1%D1%83%D0%B4%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%96_%D0%A6%D0%A3%D0%9C%D1%83._%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%97%D0%B2._1989_%D1%80._%28%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B0%D0%B3%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%A0%D0%B0%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%80%D1%81%29.jpg/250px-%D0%A0%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BD%D1%96_%D1%89%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B8_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B1%D1%83%D0%B4%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%96_%D0%A6%D0%A3%D0%9C%D1%83._%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%97%D0%B2._1989_%D1%80._%28%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B0%D0%B3%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%A0%D0%B0%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%80%D1%81%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A0%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BD%D1%96_%D1%89%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B8_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B1%D1%83%D0%B4%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%96_%D0%A6%D0%A3%D0%9C%D1%83._%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%97%D0%B2._1989_%D1%80._(%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B0%D0%B3%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%A0%D0%B0%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%80%D1%81).jpg)

Advertising on the [Central Department Store](/wiki/TsUM_Kyiv "TsUM Kyiv") in Kyiv, 1989

The Soviet [anti-alcohol campaign](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_Russian_Empire_and_the_Soviet_Union#Prohibition_from_1985_to_1988 "Prohibition in the Russian Empire and the Soviet Union"), initiated by Gorbachev in 1985, was widely perceived as a demonstration of the regime's incompetence and resulted in the spread of [drug addiction](/wiki/Drug_addiction "Drug addiction") and destruction of valuable [vineyards](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard") in Crimea and Transcarpathia. Additionally, the increase of [moonshine](/wiki/Moonshine "Moonshine") production resulting from the government bans led to a deficit of [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") in stores. Following a period of crackdown on private enterprise, in November 1986 Soviet authorities adopted the Law on Individual Labour Activities, which permitted the formation of [cooperatives](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative"), predominantly in the spheres of [catering](/wiki/Catering "Catering") and other services. By the late 1980s cooperatives in Ukraine employed 700,000 people and produced the value of almost 5 billion [rubles](/wiki/Soviet_ruble "Soviet ruble"). Functioning in a [command economy](/wiki/Command_economy "Command economy") system, cooperatives worked according to market laws and served as a school of [free enterprise](/wiki/Free_enterprise "Free enterprise") for Soviet people.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013301-302_(volume_3)-107) In 1989 a government commission headed by [Leonid Abalkin](/wiki/Leonid_Abalkin "Leonid Abalkin") proposed to decrease state involvement in the economy and initiate a transition to [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market"). Meanwhile, the increasing [devaluation](/wiki/Devaluation "Devaluation") of Soviet ruble led to the emergence of [barter](/wiki/Barter "Barter") relations between enterprises. In order to prevent outflow of goods, in late 1990 Ukrainian authorities introduced consumer [coupons](/wiki/Coupon "Coupon") for purchase of basic goods in the republic. On 3 August 1990 the [Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Ukrainian_SSR "Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian SSR") adopted the Law on Economic Sovereignty, which gave Ukrainian authorities right to define the republic's strategy of economic development.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013305-306_(volume_3)-108)

#### Establishment of sovereignty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Establishment of sovereignty")]

Further information: [1989-1991 Ukrainian revolution](/wiki/1989-1991_Ukrainian_revolution "1989-1991 Ukrainian revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Verkhovna_Rada_march_1990.svg/250px-Verkhovna_Rada_march_1990.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Verkhovna_Rada_march_1990.svg)

Composition of the Supreme Soviet of Ukraine after the March 1990 election (Communists in Red, Democratic Bloc in blue)

In March 1989 [first free elections](/wiki/1989_Soviet_Union_legislative_election "1989 Soviet Union legislative election") since [1917](/wiki/1917_Russian_Constituent_Assembly_election "1917 Russian Constituent Assembly election") took place in the Soviet Union. In Ukraine, the vote was notable due to the failure of several high-ranked Communist officials to get elected. Nevertheless, 87,8% of Ukraine's representatives in the [Congress of People's Deputies](/wiki/Congress_of_People%27s_Deputies_of_the_Soviet_Union "Congress of People's Deputies of the Soviet Union") were members of the Communist Party. At the same time, CPSU ceased to be a de-facto state institution and was forced to compete on par with other political forces. Preparing for the [following elections](/wiki/1990_Ukrainian_Supreme_Soviet_election "1990 Ukrainian Supreme Soviet election") to the Ukrainian Supreme Soviet, in November 1989 Ukrainian civic activists established the [Democratic Bloc](/wiki/Democratic_Bloc_(Ukraine) "Democratic Bloc (Ukraine)"), which included representatives of Rukh, [Memorial](/wiki/Memorial_(society) "Memorial (society)"), Ukrainian Helsinki Union and other organizations. As a result of the elections on 4 March 1990, the bloc failed to achieve victory in most of Ukraine, but received an absolute majority of votes in Lviv, Ivano-Frankivsk and Ternopil oblasts. The following months were marked with the establishment of first Ukrainian parties serving as an alternative to Communists: in April the [Ukrainian Republican Party](/wiki/Ukrainian_Republican_Party "Ukrainian Republican Party") was founded in Kyiv, and [Ukrainian Christian Democratic Party](/wiki/Ukrainian_Christian_Democratic_Party "Ukrainian Christian Democratic Party") was created in Lviv. In June the [Liberal Democratic Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_of_Ukraine "Liberal Democratic Party of Ukraine") organized its foundational congress in [Kyiv University](/wiki/Kyiv_University "Kyiv University"), and in September the [Democratic Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_Ukraine "Democratic Party of Ukraine") was organized in [Terebovlia](/wiki/Terebovlia "Terebovlia"). During the same month, the [Green Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Ukraine "Green Party of Ukraine") was officially established. Still, by early 1991 all new parties included only 30,000 members in comparison to almost 3 million people in the rows of the Communist Party of Ukraine. In February 1990 the "Democratic Platform" of the Communist Party of Ukraine constituted itself at a congress in Kharkiv. Its program promoted the abandonment of Communist ideological monopoly and supported transformation of the organization into a regular parliamentary party. After the platform failed to get support of the Central Committee, many of its members entered the [Party of Democratic Revival of Ukraine](/wiki/Party_of_Democratic_Revival_of_Ukraine "Party of Democratic Revival of Ukraine"), which constituted itself in December 1990.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013322-328_(volume_3)-109)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/%D0%9F%D1%96%D0%B4%D0%BD%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%82%D1%8F_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B4_%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%94%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BC%2C_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%96%D1%8F.JPG/250px-%D0%9F%D1%96%D0%B4%D0%BD%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%82%D1%8F_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B4_%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%94%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BC%2C_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%96%D1%8F.JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%9F%D1%96%D0%B4%D0%BD%D1%8F%D1%82%D1%82%D1%8F_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B4_%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%94%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BC,_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%96%D1%8F.JPG)

A demonstration under Ukrainian flags in Kyiv, July 1990

The opening of Ukraine's newly elected Supreme Council on 15 May 1990 was marked by fierce opposition between the Communist majority and the fraction of Democratic Bloc, known as "People's Council". In order to accommodate the opposition, majority leader [Oleksandr Moroz](/wiki/Oleksandr_Moroz "Oleksandr Moroz") allowed its representatives to establish control over seven parliamentary commissions.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013333-334_(volume_3)-110) The ongoing power struggle between Gorbachev and [Boris Yeltsin](/wiki/Boris_Yeltsin "Boris Yeltsin") in Moscow led to the adoption of pro-[sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") positions by many mebers of Ukrainian Communist nomenklatura. Following [the adoption of sovereignty declarations](/wiki/Parade_of_sovereignties "Parade of sovereignties") by parliaments of several Soviet republics, on 16 July 1990 the Supreme Soviet of Ukraine adopted its own [Declaration of State Sovereignty](/wiki/Declaration_of_State_Sovereignty_of_Ukraine "Declaration of State Sovereignty of Ukraine"), which included notions of equality and self-sufficiency in spheres of interior and foreign affairs, economy, finance and culture, proclaimed the right of free development for all ethnicities inhabiting Ukraine, and issued claims on own armed forces, police and security services. On 18 July Leonid Kravchuk was elected new head of the Supreme Council, replacing Volodymyr Ivashko. On 10 October, the parliament adopted a resolution on the return of all Ukrainian [conscripts](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") from other republics of the Soviet Union in order to continue their service in Ukraine.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013337-340_(volume_3)-111)

#### Downfall of the Communist regime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Downfall of the Communist regime")]

Following the declarations of sovereignty by republics, Gorbachev and his supporters produced a draft of the [New Union Treaty](/wiki/New_Union_Treaty "New Union Treaty"), which envisioned the republics as sovereign entities, but recognized the supremacy of the Union's law over local legislation. Rukh members opposed that project and demanded that the Supreme Council establish bilateral relations with Ukraine's neighbours. The unwillingness of the Supreme Council to introduce promised reforms led to a wave of dissatisfaction in the society, and in October 1990 a [hunger strike](/wiki/Revolution_on_Granite "Revolution on Granite") organized by students in Kyiv forced the parliament to remove [Vitaliy Masol](/wiki/Vitaliy_Masol "Vitaliy Masol") from the post of prime minister, replacing him with [Vitold Fokin](/wiki/Vitold_Fokin "Vitold Fokin"). On 23 October the Supreme Soviet abolished [Article 6](/wiki/Leading_role_of_the_party "Leading role of the party") of the Ukrainian Soviet constitution. Another important reform provided more authonomy to local government. On 17 March 1991 a [referendum](/wiki/1991_Soviet_Union_referendum "1991 Soviet Union referendum") on the preservation of the Soviet Union took place simultaneously with an all-Ukrainian survey organized by the Supreme Soviet of Ukraine in respect to the Declaration of State Sovereignty. Both the question on the preservation of USSR and the question on Ukraine's sovereignty inside of the union received a positive answer from the majority of voters.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013340-343_(volume_3)-112) On 3 July 1991 the Supreme Council introduced the title of [President of Ukraine](/wiki/President_of_Ukraine "President of Ukraine") and adopted election for that post for 1 December.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013345_(volume_3)-113)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Akt_nezalezhnosti.jpg/250px-Akt_nezalezhnosti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Akt_nezalezhnosti.jpg)

The [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Ukraine "Declaration of Independence of Ukraine"), as printed on the ballot for the [referendum on 1 December 1991](/wiki/1991_Ukrainian_independence_referendum "1991 Ukrainian independence referendum")

Following the start of the [August Coup](/wiki/1991_Soviet_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "1991 Soviet coup d'état attempt") in Moscow on 19 August 1991, [Soviet Army](/wiki/Soviet_Army "Soviet Army") commander [Valentin Varennikov](/wiki/Valentin_Varennikov "Valentin Varennikov") was dispatched to Kyiv, and informed the Supreme Soviet that any attempts to disregard the decisions of the [State Emergency Committee](/wiki/State_Committee_on_the_State_of_Emergency "State Committee on the State of Emergency") would lead to the introduction of [martial law](/wiki/Martial_law "Martial law") in the republic. Varennikov's declaration was delivered in presence of Ukrainian First Secretary [Stanislav Hurenko](/wiki/Stanislav_Hurenko "Stanislav Hurenko") and a number of generals. Speaking on television on the same day, Leonid Kravchuk declared his support for constitutional norms and democracy, warning against bloodshed, but didn't adopt a clear position. In response to the coup, democratic opposition organized protest meetings in a number of cities. After receiving information about the indecision of coup organizers in Moscow, on 20 August Kravchuk declared his intention to protect Ukraine's sovereignty. On the same day, Presidium of the Supreme Soviet of Ukriane refused to recognize orders of the State Emergency Committee. Simultaneously, regional administrations in several Ukrainian regions and Crimea recognized the committee's authority under the influence of local Communists. Following the coup's failure, on 23 August the Central Committee of the Communist Party of Ukraine issued an official condemnation of the event. On 24 August 1991, on the proposal of [Ihor Yukhnovskyi](/wiki/Ihor_Yukhnovskyi "Ihor Yukhnovskyi"), the Supreme Soviet adopted the [Declaration of Independence of Ukraine](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Ukraine "Declaration of Independence of Ukraine") and renamed the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic as **[Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine")**. On 26 August the activities of the Communist Party of Ukraine were suspended, and four days later the party was banned due to its members' participation in the coup attempt. However, former Communists remained active in political life and soon reorganized themselves into new political organizations, such as the [Socialist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_Ukraine "Socialist Party of Ukraine"). [[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013347-348_(volume_3)-114)

#### Final independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Final independence")]

Further information: [Dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Ukr_Referendum_1991.png/250px-Ukr_Referendum_1991.png)](/wiki/File:Ukr_Referendum_1991.png)

Percentage of support for Ukraine's independence by region during the 1 December 1991 referendum

A [referendum on independence](/wiki/1991_Ukrainian_independence_referendum "1991 Ukrainian independence referendum") was held in Ukraine on 1 December 1991. 92.3% of voters voted for independence nationwide. The referendum carried in the majority of all oblasts, including [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea") where 54% voted for independence, and those in [Eastern Ukraine](/wiki/Eastern_Ukraine "Eastern Ukraine") where more than 80% voted for independence. In the [1991 Ukrainian presidential election](/wiki/1991_Ukrainian_presidential_election "1991 Ukrainian presidential election") held on the same day as the independence referendum, 62 percent of voters voted for Verkhovna Rada chairman [Leonid Kravchuk](/wiki/Leonid_Kravchuk "Leonid Kravchuk"), who had been vested with presidential powers since the Supreme Soviet's declaration of independence.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996724-115) Kravchuk and the other presidential candidates all supported independence and campaigned for a “yes” vote in the independence referendum.

For most of the Soviet Union's existence, Ukraine had been second only to Russia in economic and political power, and its secession ended any realistic chance of the Soviet Union staying together even on a limited scale. 8 December 1991, Kravchuk joined his Russian and Belarusian counterparts in signing the [Belovezh Accords](/wiki/Belovezha_Accords "Belovezha Accords"), which declared that the Soviet Union had effectively ceased to exist and founded the [Commonwealth of Independent States](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States") as a quasi-replacement.[[112]](#cite_note-116) On 21 December 1991, all the former Soviet republics (except Estonia, Georgia, Lithuania and Latvia) signed the [Alma-Ata Protocol](/wiki/Alma-Ata_Protocol "Alma-Ata Protocol") which reiterated that the Soviet Union had functionally ceased to exist and formally established the CIS. The Soviet Union formally dissolved on 26 December 1991.[[113]](#cite_note-117)

## Politics and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Politics and government")]

Further information: [Constitution of the Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Ukrainian_SSR "Constitution of the Ukrainian SSR"), [Politics of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Soviet_Union "Politics of the Soviet Union"), and [Leaders of Soviet Ukraine](/wiki/Leaders_of_Soviet_Ukraine "Leaders of Soviet Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Soviet_Union_Administrative_Divisions_1989.jpg/250px-Soviet_Union_Administrative_Divisions_1989.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soviet_Union_Administrative_Divisions_1989.jpg)

Location of the Ukrainian SSR (yellow) within the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in 1954–1991

The Ukrainian SSR's system of government was based on a [one-party](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") [communist system](/wiki/State_communism "State communism") ruled by the [Communist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)"), a branch of the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") (KPSS). The republic was one of 15 constituent republics composing the Soviet Union from its entry into the union in 1922 until its dissolution in 1991. All of the political power and authority in the USSR was in the hands of Communist Party authorities, with little real power being concentrated in official government bodies and organs. In such a system, lower-level authorities directly reported to higher level authorities and so on, with the bulk of the power being held at the highest echelons of the Communist Party.[[114]](#cite_note-eou-c-118) The regime established by the Bolsheviks in Ukraine and other Soviet republics can be characterized as a form of party [oligarchy](/wiki/Oligarchy "Oligarchy").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013107_(volume_2)-119)

Originally, the legislative authority was vested in the [Congress of Soviets of Ukraine](/wiki/All-Ukrainian_Congress_of_Soviets "All-Ukrainian Congress of Soviets"), whose [Central Executive Committee](/wiki/All-Ukrainian_Central_Executive_Committee "All-Ukrainian Central Executive Committee") was for many years headed by [Grigory Petrovsky](/wiki/Grigory_Petrovsky "Grigory Petrovsky"). Soon after publishing a [Stalinist constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic#Constitution_of_1937 "Constitution of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic"), the Congress of Soviets was transformed into the [Supreme Soviet](/wiki/Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic") (and the Central Executive Committee into [its Presidium](/wiki/Presidium_of_the_Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Presidium of the Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic")), which consisted of 450 deputies.[[note 3]](#cite_note-120) The Supreme Soviet had the authority to enact legislation, amend the [constitution](/wiki/Constitutions_of_the_Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Constitutions of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic"), adopt new administrative and territorial boundaries, adopt the budget, and establish political and economic development plans.[[116]](#cite_note-eou-121) In addition, parliament also had to authority to elect the republic's executive branch, the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Ukrainian_SSR "Council of Ministers of the Ukrainian SSR") as well as the power to appoint judges to the Supreme Court. Legislative sessions were short and were conducted for only a few weeks out of the year. In spite of this, the Supreme Soviet elected the Presidium, the [Chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Verkhovna_Rada "Chairman of the Verkhovna Rada"), three deputy chairmen, a secretary, and couple of other government members to carry out the official functions and duties in between legislative sessions.[[116]](#cite_note-eou-121) Chairman of the Presidium was a powerful position in the republic's higher echelons of power, and could nominally be considered the equivalent of [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"),[[116]](#cite_note-eou-121) although most executive authority would be concentrated in the Communist Party's [politburo](/wiki/Politburo "Politburo") and [its First Secretary](/wiki/First_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_Ukraine "First Secretary of the Communist Party of Ukraine").

Full [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") was granted for all eligible citizens aged 18 and over, excluding prisoners and those deprived of freedom. Although they could not be considered free and were [of a symbolic nature](/wiki/Show_election "Show election"), elections to the Supreme Soviet were contested every five years. Nominees from electoral districts from around the republic, typically consisting of an average of 110,000 inhabitants, were directly chosen by party authorities,[[116]](#cite_note-eou-121) providing little opportunity for political change, since all political authority was directly subordinate to the higher level above it.

With the beginning of Soviet [General Secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev")'s *[perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika")* reforms towards the mid-late 1980s, electoral reform laws were passed in 1989, liberalising the nominating procedures and allowing multiple candidates to stand for election in a district. Accordingly, the [first relatively free elections](/wiki/1990_Ukrainian_Supreme_Soviet_election "1990 Ukrainian Supreme Soviet election")[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESubtelny2000[httpsarchiveorgdetailsukrainehistory00subt_0page576_576]-122) in the Ukrainian SSR were contested in March 1990. 111 deputies from the [Democratic Bloc](/wiki/Democratic_Bloc_(Ukraine) "Democratic Bloc (Ukraine)"), a loose association of small pro-Ukrainian and pro-sovereignty parties and the instrumental [People's Movement of Ukraine](/wiki/People%27s_Movement_of_Ukraine "People's Movement of Ukraine") (colloquially known as *Rukh* in Ukrainian) were elected to the parliament.[[118]](#cite_note-123) Although the Communist Party retained its majority with 331 deputies, large support for the Democratic Bloc demonstrated the people's distrust of the Communist authorities, which would eventually boil down to Ukrainian independence in 1991.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/%D0%A5%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%BC._%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%96%D0%B6%D0%B6%D1%8F._500_%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BA%D1%96%D0%B2_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D1%83_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%82%D0%B2%D1%83._1990._8.JPG/250px-%D0%A5%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%BC._%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%96%D0%B6%D0%B6%D1%8F._500_%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BA%D1%96%D0%B2_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D1%83_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%82%D0%B2%D1%83._1990._8.JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%A5%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%BC._%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%96%D0%B6%D0%B6%D1%8F._500_%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BA%D1%96%D0%B2_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D1%83_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%82%D0%B2%D1%83._1990._8.JPG)

[Anti-Soviet](/wiki/Anti-Sovietism "Anti-Sovietism") protesters with [Ukrainian flags](/wiki/Flag_of_Ukraine "Flag of Ukraine") in [Zaporizhzhia](/wiki/Zaporizhzhia "Zaporizhzhia") in 1990

Ukraine is the legal successor of the Ukrainian SSR and it stated to fulfill "those rights and duties pursuant to international agreements of Union SSR which do not contradict the [Constitution of Ukraine](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine") and interests of the Republic" on 5 October 1991.[[119]](#cite_note-124) After [Ukrainian independence](/wiki/Ukrainian_independence "Ukrainian independence") the Ukrainian SSR's parliament was changed from *Supreme Soviet* to its current name [Verkhovna Rada](/wiki/Verkhovna_Rada "Verkhovna Rada"), retaining its status as the country's legislature.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi2010[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidTA1zVKTTsXUCpgPA722_722]-11)[[120]](#cite_note-125) Ukraine also has refused to recognize exclusive Russian claims to succession of the Soviet Union and claimed such status for Ukraine as well, which was stated in Articles 7 and 8 of [On Legal Succession of Ukraine](/wiki/Law_on_the_Succession_of_Ukraine "Law on the Succession of Ukraine"), issued in 1991. Following independence, Ukraine has continued to pursue claims against the Russian Federation in foreign courts, seeking to recover its share of the foreign property that was owned by the Soviet Union. It also retained [its seat in the United Nations](/wiki/Ukraine_and_the_United_Nations "Ukraine and the United Nations"), held since 1945.

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

See also: [Foreign relations of Ukraine](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Ukraine "Foreign relations of Ukraine") and [International membership of Ukraine](/wiki/International_membership_of_Ukraine "International membership of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Treaty_of_Riga.jpg/250px-Treaty_of_Riga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Riga.jpg)

Polish text of the 1921 Treaty of Riga, recognizing the independence of Soviet Ukraine and [Belarus](/wiki/Belarusian_SSR "Belarusian SSR")

During the first years of its existence, Ukrainian SSR was the only Soviet republic along with Soviet Russia to establish its own foreign contacts. Following the signing of a union treaty with Russian SFSR on 28 December 1920, the government of [Christian Rakovsky](/wiki/Christian_Rakovsky "Christian Rakovsky") signed an agreement on cooperation with the [Menshevik](/wiki/Menshevik "Menshevik") [Georgian Democratic Republic](/wiki/Georgian_Democratic_Republic "Georgian Democratic Republic"), and on 14 February 1921 Soviet Ukraine established diplomatic ties with [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"). During the [talks in Riga](/wiki/Treaty_of_Riga "Treaty of Riga") in 1921 Soviet Ukrainian representative [Emmanuel Kviring](/wiki/Emmanuel_Kviring "Emmanuel Kviring") supported the idea of establishing the republic's embassies in the capitals of [great powers](/wiki/Great_powers "Great powers"), as well as in Poland, [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), [Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") and [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"). In October of the same year [Alexander Shumsky](/wiki/Alexander_Shumsky "Alexander Shumsky") was appointed ambassador of Soviet Ukraine in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"). In 1921-1922 Ukrainian SSR signed peace treaties with [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") and [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), and established friendly relations with [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"). Soviet Ukrainian [trade missions](/wiki/Trade_mission "Trade mission") were established in several European cities. However, starting from 1922 Ukrainian SSR was forced to cease the activities of its diplomatic organs under pressure from [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"). At the [Lausanne Conference](/wiki/1922-1923_Lausanne_Conference "1922-1923 Lausanne Conference") representatives of Soviet Ukraine took part in negotiations as part of a common delegation with representatives of Soviet Russia and [Soviet Georgia](/wiki/Soviet_Georgia "Soviet Georgia"), headed by [Georgy Chicherin](/wiki/Georgy_Chicherin "Georgy Chicherin"). In September 1923 the diplomatic apparatus of Ukrainian SSR ceased its existence, and its consulates and services were incorporated into the [all-Union commissariat](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Soviet_Union) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Soviet Union)").[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013119-126_(volume_2)-126)

During the following decades, the Ukrainian SSR, along with the rest of the 15 republics, had virtually no say in their own foreign affairs. However, since 1944, the Ukrainian SSR was permitted to establish bilateral relations with countries and maintain its own standing army.[[114]](#cite_note-eou-c-118) This clause was used to permit the republic's membership in the United Nations, alongside the Byelorussian SSR. Accordingly, representatives from the "Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic" and 50 other states founded the UN on 24 October 1945. In effect, this provided the Soviet Union (a permanent [Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") member with veto powers) with another two votes in the [General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly").[[note 4]](#cite_note-127) The latter aspect of the 1944 clauses was never fulfilled and the republic's defense matters were managed by the [Soviet Armed Forces](/wiki/Soviet_Armed_Forces "Soviet Armed Forces") and the Defense Ministry. Another right that was granted but never used until 1991 was the right of the Soviet republics to secede from the union,[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESubtelny2000421-128) which was codified in each of the [Soviet constitutions](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Constitution of the Soviet Union"). Accordingly, Article 69 of the Constitution of the Ukrainian SSR stated: "The Ukrainian SSR retains the right to willfully secede from the USSR."[[123]](#cite_note-129) However, a republic's theoretical secession from the union was virtually impossible and unrealistic[[114]](#cite_note-eou-c-118) in many ways until after Gorbachev's [perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika") reforms.

The Ukrainian SSR was a member of the [UN Economic and Social Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_and_Social_Council "United Nations Economic and Social Council"), [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"), [International Labour Organization](/wiki/International_Labour_Organization "International Labour Organization"), [Universal Postal Union](/wiki/Universal_Postal_Union "Universal Postal Union"), [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization"), [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), [International Telecommunication Union](/wiki/International_Telecommunication_Union "International Telecommunication Union"), [United Nations Economic Commission for Europe](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_Commission_for_Europe "United Nations Economic Commission for Europe"), [World Intellectual Property Organization](/wiki/World_Intellectual_Property_Organization "World Intellectual Property Organization") and the [International Atomic Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Atomic_Energy_Agency "International Atomic Energy Agency"). It was not separately a member of the [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact"), [Comecon](/wiki/Comecon "Comecon"), the [World Federation of Trade Unions](/wiki/World_Federation_of_Trade_Unions "World Federation of Trade Unions") and the [World Federation of Democratic Youth](/wiki/World_Federation_of_Democratic_Youth "World Federation of Democratic Youth"), and since 1949, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee").

On 23 June 1954, the civilian [oil tanker](/wiki/Oil_tanker "Oil tanker") *[Tuapse](/wiki/Capture_of_the_Tuapse "Capture of the Tuapse")* of the [Black Sea Shipping Company](/wiki/Black_Sea_Shipping_Company "Black Sea Shipping Company") based in [Odessa](/wiki/Odessa "Odessa") was hijacked by a fleet of [Republic of China Navy](/wiki/Republic_of_China_Navy "Republic of China Navy") in the [high sea](/wiki/International_waters "International waters") of *19°35′N, 120°39′E*, west of [Balintang Channel](/wiki/Balintang_Channel "Balintang Channel") near [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), whereas the 49 Ukrainian, Russian and Moldovan crew were detained by the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") regime in various terms up to 34 years in [captivity](/wiki/Captivity "Captivity") with three deaths.[[124]](#cite_note-130)[[125]](#cite_note-131)[[126]](#cite_note-132)

## Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Kharkov_1981_Vedmedik.jpg/250px-Kharkov_1981_Vedmedik.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kharkov_1981_Vedmedik.jpg)

Central [Kharkov](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") in 1981

Main article: [Administrative divisions of the Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_Ukrainian_SSR "Administrative divisions of the Ukrainian SSR")

Legally, the Soviet Union and its fifteen union republics constituted a [federal system](/wiki/Federal_system "Federal system"), but the country was functionally a highly centralised state, with all major decision-making taking place in the [Kremlin](/wiki/Kremlin "Kremlin"), the capital and seat of government of the country. The constituent republics were essentially [unitary states](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"), with lower levels of power being directly subordinate to higher ones. Throughout its 72-year existence, the administrative divisions of the Ukrainian SSR changed numerous times, often incorporating regional reorganisation and annexation on the part of Soviet authorities during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

The most common administrative division was the [oblast](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast") (province), of which there were 25 upon the republic's independence from the Soviet Union in 1991. Provinces were further subdivided into [raions](/wiki/Raion "Raion") (districts) which numbered 490. The rest of the administrative division within the provinces consisted of cities, [urban-type settlements](/wiki/Urban-type_settlement "Urban-type settlement"), and villages. Cities in the Ukrainian SSR were a separate exception, which could either be subordinate to either the provincial authorities themselves or the district authorities of which they were the administrative center. Two cities, the capital [Kiev](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), and [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol") (which hosted a large Soviet Navy base in Crimea), were uniquely designated "cities with special status." This meant that they were directly subordinate to the central Ukrainian SSR authorities and not the provincial authorities surrounding them.

### Historical formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Historical formation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Map_of_Ukrainian_oblasts_%281946%E2%80%931954%29.png/250px-Map_of_Ukrainian_oblasts_%281946%E2%80%931954%29.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ukrainian_oblasts_(1946%E2%80%931954).png)

The 25 [oblasts](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast") of Ukraine through 1946 to 1954. [Crimea](/wiki/Crimean_Oblast "Crimean Oblast") would be transferred in 1954 and the [Drohobych](/wiki/Drohobych_Oblast "Drohobych Oblast") and [Izmail](/wiki/Izmail_Oblast "Izmail Oblast") oblasts would be absorbed by, respectively, the [Lvov](/wiki/Lviv_Oblast "Lviv Oblast") and [Odessa](/wiki/Odesa_Oblast "Odesa Oblast") oblasts.

However, the history of administrative divisions in the republic was not so clear cut. At the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in 1918, Ukraine was invaded by [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Soviet_Russia "Soviet Russia") as the Russian puppet government of the Ukrainian SSR and without official declaration it ignited the [Ukrainian–Soviet War](/wiki/Ukrainian%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Ukrainian–Soviet War") [*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic#Dubious "Talk:Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Government of the [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR") from very start was managed by the [Communist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)") that was created in Moscow and was originally formed out of the Bolshevik organisational centers in Ukraine. Occupying the eastern city of [Kharkov](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv"), the Soviet forces chose it as the republic's seat of government, colloquially named in the media as "Kharkov – Pervaya Stolitsa (the first capital)" with implication to the era of [Soviet regime](/wiki/Soviet_regime "Soviet regime").[[127]](#cite_note-133) Kharkov was also the city where the first Soviet Ukrainian government was created in 1917 with strong support from Russian SFSR authorities. However, in 1934, the capital was moved from Kharkov to [Kiev](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), which remains the capital of Ukraine today.

During the 1930s, there were significant numbers of ethnic minorities living within the Ukrainian SSR. National Districts were formed as separate territorial-administrative units within higher-level provincial authorities. Districts were established for the republic's three largest minority groups, which were the Jews, [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians"), and [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people").[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi2007229-134) Other ethnic groups, however, were allowed to petition the government for their own national autonomy. In 1924 on the territory of Ukrainian SSR was formed the [Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Moldavian_Autonomous_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic"). Upon the 1940 conquest of Bessarabia and Bukovina by Soviet troops the Moldavian ASSR was passed to the newly formed [Moldavian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Moldavian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Moldavian Soviet Socialist Republic"), while [Budzhak](/wiki/Budjak "Budjak") and Bukovina were secured by the Ukrainian SSR. Following the creation of the Ukrainian SSR significant numbers of ethnic Ukrainians found themselves living outside the Ukrainian SSR.[[129]](#cite_note-ukrainianweek43727-135) In the 1920s the Ukrainian SSR was forced to cede several territories to Russia in [Severia](/wiki/Severia "Severia"), [Sloboda Ukraine](/wiki/Sloboda_Ukraine "Sloboda Ukraine") and Azov littoral including such cities like [Belgorod](/wiki/Belgorod "Belgorod"), [Taganrog](/wiki/Taganrog "Taganrog") and [Starodub](/wiki/Starodub "Starodub"). In the 1920s the administration of the Ukrainian SSR insisted in vain on reviewing the border between the Ukrainian Soviet Republics and the [Russian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") based on the 1926 [First All-Union Census of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Census_(1926) "Soviet Census (1926)") that showed that 4.5 millions of Ukrainians were living on Russian territories bordering Ukraine.[[129]](#cite_note-ukrainianweek43727-135) A forced end to [Ukrainisation](/wiki/Ukrainization "Ukrainization") in southern Russian Soviet Republic led to a massive decline of reported Ukrainians in these regions in the [1937 Soviet Census](/wiki/Soviet_Census_(1937) "Soviet Census (1937)").[[129]](#cite_note-ukrainianweek43727-135)

Upon signing of the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"), Nazi Germany and Soviet Union partitioned [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") and its Eastern Borderlands were secured by the Soviet buffer republics with Ukraine securing the territory of Eastern Galicia. The Soviet September Polish campaign in Soviet propaganda was portrayed as the Golden September for Ukrainians, given the unification of Ukrainian lands on both banks of [Zbruch River](/wiki/Zbruch "Zbruch"), until then the border between the Soviet Union and the Polish communities inhabited by Ukrainian speaking families.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Economy")]

Further information: [Economy of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Soviet_Union "Economy of the Soviet Union")

### Before 1945

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Before 1945")]

At the onset of Soviet Ukraine, having largely inherited conditions from the Tsarist Empire, one of the biggest exporters of wheat in the world, the Ukrainian economy was still centered around [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), with over 90% of the workforce being peasants.[[130]](#cite_note-136) In the 1920s, Soviet policies in Ukraine attached importance to developing the economy. The initial agenda, [War Communism](/wiki/War_Communism "War Communism"), had prescribed total communisation and appropriation per quota of food from the people by force[[131]](#cite_note-137) - further economic damage and a [1921–1923 famine in Ukraine](/wiki/1921%E2%80%931923_famine_in_Ukraine "1921–1923 famine in Ukraine") claiming up to one million lives ensued. With the [New Economic Policy](/wiki/New_Economic_Policy "New Economic Policy") and the partial introduction of free markets, an economic recovery followed.

During the 1920s, the majority of Ukraine's economic enterprises was subordinated to the union centre.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013156_(volume_2)-138) In 1921 Soviet Ukraine's authorities introduced the system of [khozraschet](/wiki/Khozraschet "Khozraschet"), which turned enterprises into autonomous subjects of the economy. Such enterprises were united into [trusts](/wiki/Trust_(business) "Trust (business)"), which represented separate branches of industry.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013158-160_(volume_2)-139) At the same time, trusts were barred from paying taxes from their [fixed assets](/wiki/Fixed_assets "Fixed assets"), and were instead obliged to deliver a specially introduced "industrial tax", which increased their dependence on the state budget. The economic system was distorted due to the prioritization of non-consumer production by the regime.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013164-165_(volume_2)-140) The period of NEP saw the revival of credit and financial institutions, a currency reform and the organization of state trade and cooperation. Starting from 1925 the economic policies experienced a turn towards [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") and [collectivization](/wiki/Collectivization "Collectivization").[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013169-170_(volume_2)-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/%D0%A1%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B2%D0%BE_%D0%94%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B3%D1%8D%D1%81%D0%B0.jpg/250px-%D0%A1%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B2%D0%BE_%D0%94%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B3%D1%8D%D1%81%D0%B0.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A1%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B2%D0%BE_%D0%94%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B3%D1%8D%D1%81%D0%B0.jpg)

Construction of the [Dnieper Hydroelectric Power Plant](/wiki/Dnieper_Hydroelectric_Station "Dnieper Hydroelectric Station") in [Zaporizhzhia](/wiki/Zaporizhzhia "Zaporizhzhia"), 1931

After the death of Lenin and the consolidation of his power, Stalin was determined to industrialisation and reversed policy again. As heavy industry and wheat exports boomed, common people in rural areas were bearing a cost. Gradually escalating measures, from raised taxes, dispossession of property, and [forced deportations into Siberia](/wiki/Special_settlements_in_the_Soviet_Union#Deportations_of_1928–1939 "Special settlements in the Soviet Union") culminated in extremely high grain delivery quotas. Even though there is no evidence that agricultural yield could not feed the population at the time, four million Ukrainians were starved to death during the 1932–1933 [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor"), while Moscow exported over a million tonnes of grain to the West,[[136]](#cite_note-142) decimating the population.[[137]](#cite_note-143)

In the aftermath of collectivization, the Soviet regime achieved a mode of coexistence with Ukraine's peasants through the employment of women in order to compensate for demographic losses during the famine, as well as [mechanization](/wiki/Mechanization "Mechanization") of agriculture and the introduction of a [passport](/wiki/Passport "Passport") system.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013543-552_(volume_2)-144) In the industrial sphere, Soviet Ukraine during the 1930s was dominated by [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy") and [food industry](/wiki/Food_industry "Food industry"). Between 1928 and 1937 the industrial growth in Ukrainian SSR achieved record numbers worldwide. The number of workers grew almost threefold.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013563_(volume_2)-145) For the first time in history, by the late 1930s, less than half of Soviet Ukraine's population was employed in agriculture. During that time the republic was Europe's second biggest producer of [pig iron](/wiki/Pig_iron "Pig iron") and took the fourth place worldwide in [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") production. At the same time, the development of Ukraine's industry repeated pre-revolutionary patterns, with most of its centers being concentrated in the [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas")-[Dnieper](/wiki/Dnieper "Dnieper") region.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013574-575_(volume_2)-146)

### After 1945

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: After 1945")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Moscow_VDNKh_Ukraine_Pavilion_asv2018-08.jpg/250px-Moscow_VDNKh_Ukraine_Pavilion_asv2018-08.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moscow_VDNKh_Ukraine_Pavilion_asv2018-08.jpg)

Pavilion of Ukraine at the [All-Soviet Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Exhibition_of_Achievements_of_National_Economy "Exhibition of Achievements of National Economy") in Moscow

#### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

In 1945, agricultural production stood at only 40 percent of the 1940 level, even though the republic's territorial expansion had "increased the amount of [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land")".[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996692-147) In contrast to the remarkable growth in the industrial sector,[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996692–693-148) agriculture continued in Ukraine, as in the rest of the Soviet Union, to function as the economy's [Achilles heel](/wiki/Achilles_heel "Achilles heel"). Despite the human toll of [collectivisation of agriculture](/wiki/Collectivization "Collectivization") in the Soviet Union, especially in Ukraine,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Soviet planners still believed in the effectiveness of collective farming. The old system was reestablished; the numbers of [collective farms](/wiki/Kolkhoz "Kolkhoz") in Ukraine increased from 28 thousand in 1940 to 33 thousand in 1949, comprising 45 million hectares; the numbers of [state farms](/wiki/Sovkhoz "Sovkhoz") barely increased, standing at 935 in 1950, comprising 12.1 million hectares. By the end of the [Fourth Five-Year Plan](/wiki/Fourth_Five-Year_Plan_(Soviet_Union) "Fourth Five-Year Plan (Soviet Union)") (in 1950) and the [Fifth Five-Year Plan](/wiki/Fifth_Five-Year_Plan_(Soviet_Union) "Fifth Five-Year Plan (Soviet Union)") (in 1955), agricultural output still stood far lower than the 1940 level. The slow changes in agriculture can be explained by the low productivity in collective farms, and by bad weather-conditions, which the Soviet planning system could not effectively respond to. Grain for human consumption in the post-war years decreased, this in turn led to frequent and severe food shortages.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996693-149)

The increase of [Soviet agricultural production](/wiki/Agriculture_of_the_Soviet_Union "Agriculture of the Soviet Union") was tremendous, however, the Soviet Ukrainians still experienced food shortages due to the inefficiencies of a [highly centralised economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy"). During the peak of Soviet-Ukrainian agriculture output in the 1950s and early-to-mid-1960s, human consumption in Ukraine, and in the rest of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), actually experienced short intervals of decrease. There are many reasons for this inefficiency, but its origins can be traced back to the single-purchaser and -producer [market system](/wiki/Market_system "Market system") set up by [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin").[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996706-150)[*[need quotation to verify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Khrushchev tried to improve the agricultural situation in the Soviet Union by expanding the total crop size – for instance, in the Ukrainian SSR alone "the amount of land planted with [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize") grew by 600 percent". At the height of this policy, between 1959 and 1963, one-third of Ukrainian [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land") grew this crop. This policy decreased the total production of wheat and [rye](/wiki/Rye "Rye"); Khrushchev had anticipated this, and the production of wheat and rye moved to [Soviet Central Asia](/wiki/Soviet_Central_Asia "Soviet Central Asia")[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] as part of the [Virgin Lands Campaign](/wiki/Virgin_Lands_Campaign "Virgin Lands Campaign"). Khrushchev's agricultural policy failed, and in 1963 the Soviet Union had to import food from abroad. The total level of agricultural productivity in Ukraine decreased sharply during this period, but recovered in the 1970s and 1980s during [Leonid Brezhnev](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev")'s rule.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996708-91) A major role in Ukraine's agriculture during Soviet times was played by personal land plots, which were confiscated from peasant during Khrushchev's rule. Following their restoration to villagers by the late 1960s, they produced one-quarter of all vegetables and one-third of the total meat, lard, milk and fruit production, as well as more than half of all potatoes.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013258_(volume_3)-151) The presence of such plots in Ukraine ameliorated the [deficit of goods](/wiki/Shortage_economy "Shortage economy") in the republic during the later years of Soviet rule, when rapid [urbanisation](/wiki/Urbanisation "Urbanisation") produced an additional stress on agricultural producers.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013267_(volume_3)-152)

In 1986 an attempt to reform Soviet agriculture was made through the introduction of Agroproms, which were tasked with controlling the production of agricultural goods. This system failed to bring results, and supply difficulties continued to increase. In March 1988 collective farmers were allowed to rent land plots for a period of up to 50 years and received control over goods produced in this way. However, local administration hindered the introduction of that reform, being supported by the majority of peasants themselves.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013304_(volume_3)-153)

#### Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Industry")]

During the [post-war](/wiki/Post-war "Post-war") years, Ukraine's industrial productivity doubled its pre-war level.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996705-154) In 1945 industrial output totalled only 26 percent of the 1940 level. The Soviet Union introduced the Fourth Five-Year Plan in 1946. The Fourth Five-Year Plan would prove to be a remarkable success, and can be likened to the "[wonders of West German](/wiki/Wirtschaftswunder "Wirtschaftswunder") and Japanese reconstruction", but without foreign capital; the Soviet reconstruction is historically an impressive[*[opinion](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view/FAQ#Assert_facts,_not_opinions "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view/FAQ")*] achievement. In 1950 industrial gross output had already surpassed 1940-levels. While the Soviet régime still emphasised [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry") over [light industry](/wiki/Light_industry "Light industry"), the light-industry sector also grew. The increase in capital investment and the expansion of the labour force also benefited Ukraine's economic recovery.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996692-147) In the prewar years, 15.9 percent of the Soviet budget went to Ukraine, in 1950, during the Fourth Five-Year Plan this had increased to 19.3 percent. The workforce had increased from 1.2 million in 1945 to 2.9 million in 1955; an increase of 33.2 percent over the 1940-level.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996692-147) The result of this remarkable growth was that by 1955 Ukraine was producing 2.2 times more than in 1940, and the republic had become one of the leading producers of certain commodities in Europe. Ukraine was the largest per-capita producer in Europe of [pig iron](/wiki/Pig_iron "Pig iron") and sugar, and the second-largest per-capita producer of steel and of iron ore, and was the third largest per-capita producer of coal in Europe.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996693-149)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Chernobyl_-_power_plant_-_reactor_4_02.jpg/250px-Chernobyl_-_power_plant_-_reactor_4_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chernobyl_-_power_plant_-_reactor_4_02.jpg)

Site of the [Chernobyl nuclear disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster")

From 1965 until the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") in 1991, industrial growth in Ukraine decreased, and by the 1970s it [started to stagnate](/wiki/Era_of_Stagnation "Era of Stagnation"). Significant economic decline did not become apparent before the 1970s. During the [Fifth Five-Year Plan](/wiki/Fifth_Five-Year_Plan_(Soviet_Union) "Fifth Five-Year Plan (Soviet Union)") (1951–1955), industrial development in Ukraine grew by 13.5 percent, while during the [Eleventh Five-Year Plan](/wiki/Eleventh_five-year_plan_(Soviet_Union) "Eleventh five-year plan (Soviet Union)") (1981–1985) industry grew by a relatively modest 3.5 percent. The double-digit growth seen in all branches of the economy in the [post-war](/wiki/Post-war "Post-war") years had disappeared by the 1980s, entirely replaced by low growth-figures. An ongoing problem throughout the republic's existence was the planners' emphasis on heavy industry over [consumer goods](/wiki/Consumer_goods_in_the_Soviet_Union "Consumer goods in the Soviet Union").[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996705-154) Another issue was the wear of production facilities due to insufficient modernization expenditures by the government.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013257_(volume_3)-155)

The urbanisation of Ukrainian society in the post-war years led to an increase in [energy consumption](/wiki/World_energy_resources_and_consumption "World energy resources and consumption"). Between 1956 and 1972, to meet this increasing demand, the government built five [water reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir") along the [Dnieper River](/wiki/Dnieper "Dnieper"). Aside from improving Soviet-Ukrainian [water transport](/wiki/Water_transport "Water transport"), the reservoirs became the sites for new [power stations](/wiki/Power_stations "Power stations"), and [hydroelectric energy](/wiki/Hydroelectric_energy "Hydroelectric energy") flourished in Ukraine in consequence. The [natural-gas industry](/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry") flourished as well, and Ukraine became the site of the first post-war production of gas in the Soviet Union; by the 1960s Ukraine's biggest gas field was producing 30 percent of the USSR's total gas production. The government was not able to meet the people's ever-increasing demand for energy consumption, but by the 1970s, the Soviet government had conceived an intensive nuclear power program. According to the Eleventh Five-Year Plan, the Soviet government would build 8 [nuclear power plants](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant") in Ukraine by 1989. As a result of these efforts, Ukraine became highly diversified in energy consumption.[[150]](#cite_note-156)

On 30 June 1987 the Law on State Enterprise was adopted by the [Supreme Soviet of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Soviet_Union "Supreme Soviet of the Soviet Union"), providing separate enterprises autonomy in planning, supplies and establishment of prices. In some spheres, it was even allowed to established trade contacts with foreign firms, which for the first time broke the foreign trade monopoly introduced under Lenin.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013303_(volume_3)-157)

## Culture, science and religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Culture, science and religion")]

Further information: [Religion in the Soviet Union](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Soviet_Union "Religion in the Soviet Union") and [Russification of Ukraine § Soviet period](/wiki/Russification_of_Ukraine#Soviet_period "Russification of Ukraine")

### Stalinist era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Stalinist era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/345%D0%90%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B8%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%B3%D0%B8%D0%BE%D0%B7%D0%BD%D1%8B%D0%B5_%D0%BB%D0%BE%D0%B7%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B3%D0%B8_%D0%BA_%D0%9F%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%85%D0%B5._%D0%A7%D1%83%D0%B3%D1%83%D0%B5%D0%B2_%D0%B2_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%BC%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B5%2C_%D0%A5%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BB%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8C%2C_%D0%A3%D0%A1%D0%A1%D0%A0%2C_%D0%90%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C_1931_%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%B0.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:345%D0%90%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B8%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%B3%D0%B8%D0%BE%D0%B7%D0%BD%D1%8B%D0%B5_%D0%BB%D0%BE%D0%B7%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B3%D0%B8_%D0%BA_%D0%9F%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%85%D0%B5._%D0%A7%D1%83%D0%B3%D1%83%D0%B5%D0%B2_%D0%B2_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%BC%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B5,_%D0%A5%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BB%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8C,_%D0%A3%D0%A1%D0%A1%D0%A0,_%D0%90%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C_1931_%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%B0.jpg)

Pupils of a children's commune in [Chuhuiv](/wiki/Chuhuiv "Chuhuiv") writing anti-religious slogans on a blackboard, 1931

In 1930 compulsory [primary education](/wiki/Primary_education "Primary education") in the Soviet Union was introduced by a decree of the Council of People's Commissars.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013606_(volume_2)-158) After 1933, the policies of korenization were replaced with [Russification](/wiki/Russification_of_Ukraine "Russification of Ukraine"), which became especially prominent in the educational sphere. The Soviet history course in schools was created on the base of Russian history courses from [imperial times](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire").[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013608-609_(volume_2)-159)

During the 1930s, numerous Ukrainian scientific and cultural activists were subjected to state-organized political repressions. More than half of the members of the [Writers' Union of Ukraine](/wiki/National_Writers%27_Union_of_Ukraine "National Writers' Union of Ukraine") [were repressed](/wiki/Executed_Renaissance "Executed Renaissance") on trumped-up charges between 1934 and 1938. Many were imprisoned in [concentration camps](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag") and executed, while some, like [Mykola Khvylyovy](/wiki/Mykola_Khvylyovy "Mykola Khvylyovy"), committed [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide") due to persecution by authorities.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013613-615_(volume_2)-160)

Starting from the second half of 1920s Kyiv became a major centre of [cinematography](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography"), with the local cinema studios producing several renowned films such as *[Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_(1929_film) "Arsenal (1929 film)")* and *[Earth](/wiki/Earth_(1930_film) "Earth (1930 film)")* by [Alexander Dovzhenko](/wiki/Alexander_Dovzhenko "Alexander Dovzhenko"). After a conflict with [Mykola Skrypnyk](/wiki/Mykola_Skrypnyk "Mykola Skrypnyk"), Dovzhenko moved to Moscow and received the protection of Stalin, producing propaganda movies for his regime. A significant development in Ukrainian [arts](/wiki/Arts "Arts") was the school created by [Mykhailo Boychuk](/wiki/Mykhailo_Boychuk "Mykhailo Boychuk"), who was in 1936 arrested by the Soviet authorities and later executed. Despite repressions, the number of Ukrainian [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia") saw an increase during the first decades of Soviet rule.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013615-616_(volume_2)-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/%D0%A0%D1%83%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%89%D0%B0_%D1%81%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%83_%D0%9C%D0%B8%D1%85%D0%B0%D0%B9%D0%BB%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%B7%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%80%D1%85%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D1%80%D1%8F_%D0%BF%D1%96%D1%81%D0%BB%D1%8F_%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B4%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D1%83_14_%D1%81%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BF%D0%BD%D1%8F_1937_%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BA%D1%83.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A0%D1%83%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%89%D0%B0_%D1%81%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%83_%D0%9C%D0%B8%D1%85%D0%B0%D0%B9%D0%BB%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%B7%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%80%D1%85%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D1%80%D1%8F_%D0%BF%D1%96%D1%81%D0%BB%D1%8F_%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B4%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D1%83_14_%D1%81%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BF%D0%BD%D1%8F_1937_%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BA%D1%83.jpg)

Ruins of St. Michael's Monastery in Kyiv after its demolition in August 1937

Many churches and synagogues were destroyed during the existence of the Ukrainian SSR.[[156]](#cite_note-162) By 1937, only 850 Christian places of worship continued to function in the republic compared to approximately 10,000 less than a decade earlier. Many churches were closed and transformed into clubs, meanwhile the [St. Sophia Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Sophia_Cathedral,_Kyiv "Saint Sophia Cathedral, Kyiv") and [Monastery of the Caves](/wiki/Kyiv_Pechersk_Lavra "Kyiv Pechersk Lavra") in Kyiv were turned into museums of [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism"). In 1930 the [Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Autocephalous_Orthodox_Church "Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church") was dissolved by authorities after accusations of [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism"). In 1937 [St. Michael's Golden-Domed Monastery](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Golden-Domed_Monastery "St. Michael's Golden-Domed Monastery") in Kyiv was demolished during construction works. Nevertheless, according to materials of a census performed during the same year, over 50% of Ukrainian SSR's population declared themselves to be religious.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013616-617_(volume_2)-163)

During the Second World War, the Soviet regime toned down its propaganda of [class struggle](/wiki/Class_struggle "Class struggle"), and started appealing to heroic past of the Russians and other peoples of the USSR in fight against external enemies. Starting from the beginning of German invasion, Ukrainian historical figures such as [Daniel of Galicia](/wiki/Daniel_of_Galicia "Daniel of Galicia") and [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky") were lionized as great patriots and fighters against German aggressors, although neither of the two ever fought against Germany. This practice demonstrated the ability of Communism and Nationalism to coexist inside of a single system. In September 1943 Stalin agreed to restore the position of [patriarch](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch") of the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"). In order to rehabilitate the popular image of the regime, several prominent cultural figures, such as [Mykola Bazhan](/wiki/Mykola_Bazhan "Mykola Bazhan") and [Pavlo Tychyna](/wiki/Pavlo_Tychyna "Pavlo Tychyna"), were appointed to serve in the government of Ukrainian SSR. Ukrainian historians and publishers evacuated from the warzone engaged in production of popular works and propaganda, promoting Ukrainian Soviet patriotism. In October 1943 [Order of Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Order_of_Bogdan_Khmelnitsky_(Soviet_Union) "Order of Bogdan Khmelnitsky (Soviet Union)") was established, and the ancient city of [Pereyaslav](/wiki/Pereiaslav "Pereiaslav") was renamed in honour of the [hetman](/wiki/Hetman "Hetman"). At the same time, the official ideology continued to promote the image of Russians as the "elder brother".[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький201392-99_(volume_3)-164)

Following the establishment of Soviet rule over Western Ukraine, the [Stalinist](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") regime took a decision to eliminate the [Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church"). In March 1946 a [synod](/wiki/Synod_of_Lviv_(1946) "Synod of Lviv (1946)") in Lviv proclaimed the abolition of the 1596 [Union of Brest](/wiki/Union_of_Brest "Union of Brest") and declared the incorporation of Greek Catholics by the Russian Orthodox Church. After the murder of [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") [Theodore Romzha](/wiki/Theodore_Romzha "Theodore Romzha") in 1947, a similar operation was performed in respect to the [Ruthenian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ruthenian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ruthenian Greek Catholic Church") in Transcarpathia. As a result, by 1948 more than 62% of all Russian Orthodox parishes were located in Ukraine. Nevertheless, the closures of churches by the government continued, officially on the wishes of local population.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013145-147_(volume_3)-165)

The postwar era saw a government-organized crackdown on some branches of science, most notably [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"), which was declared unscientific under the influence of [Trofim Lysenko](/wiki/Trofim_Lysenko "Trofim Lysenko")'s ideas. Starting from 1946, Stalin's chief ideologist [Andrey Zhdanov](/wiki/Andrey_Zhdanov "Andrey Zhdanov") initiated a campaign against "bourgeois nationalism", which reached its apogee in 1951, when *[Pravda](/wiki/Pravda "Pravda")* accused [Volodymyr Sosyura](/wiki/Volodymyr_Sosyura "Volodymyr Sosyura")'s wartime poem *Love Ukraine* of "nationalism".
