Gradual rehabilitation of Ukrianian cultural figures who had fallen victim to the campaign started only after Stalin's death in 1953.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013150-153_(volume_3)-166)

### Khrushchev and Brezhnev periods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Khrushchev and Brezhnev periods")]

The liberalization of political life during the Khrushchev Thaw led to the spread of Western cultural influences in the Soviet Union. In Ukraine, translations of previously banned authors such as [Albert Camus](/wiki/Albert_Camus "Albert Camus"), [Franz Kafka](/wiki/Franz_Kafka "Franz Kafka"), [Erich Maria Remarque](/wiki/Erich_Maria_Remarque "Erich Maria Remarque") and [Ernest Hemingway](/wiki/Ernest_Hemingway "Ernest Hemingway") became possible with the reestablishment of *[Vsesvit](/wiki/Vsesvit "Vsesvit")* magazine in 1958. In 1959 students in Kyiv organized the performance of [koliadky](/wiki/Koliadka "Koliadka"), traditional Ukrainian [Christmas carols](/wiki/Christmas_carol "Christmas carol"), and were soon permitted to create a cultural club under the aegis of local [Komsomol](/wiki/Komsomol "Komsomol"). The club also engaged in social activities, among others demanding to establish a memorial to victims of Stalinist repression at [Bykivnia](/wiki/Bykivnia "Bykivnia"). Similar clubs were established in other cities of Ukriane, contributing to the emergence of [Sixtiers](/wiki/Sixtiers "Sixtiers") movement.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013191-194_(volume_3)-167)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%961959%E2%80%942001.gif/330px-%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%961959%E2%80%942001.gif)](/wiki/File:%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%961959%E2%80%942001.gif)

During the post-WW2 period, the Russification of ethnic Ukrainians,[[162]](#cite_note-168) as well as the mass immigration of ethnic Russians,[[163]](#cite_note-169) significantly changed the ethnic and linguistic composition of Ukraine's population. The change was especially evident in Donbas, where Ukrainian speakers became a minority.

Following a short period of cooperation with the Russian Orthodox Church, in 1958 Khrushchev's government announced the beginning of a new [anti-religious campaign](/wiki/USSR_anti-religious_campaign_(1958-1964) "USSR anti-religious campaign (1958-1964)"). By 1964, more than half of Ukraine's religious communities lost their churches, and two-thirds of all monasteries were shut down by authorities. Nevertheless, the vast majority of Ukrainian population in different parts of the republic continued to follow religious rites.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013197-199_(volume_3)-170) In 1958 the theses published by the Central Committee of the Communist Party promoted the [facultative](/wiki/Facultative "Facultative") status of Ukrainian language in schools with simultaneous compulsory teaching of Russian. This proposal produced a backlash among Ukraine's cultural elite. Nevertheless, between 1959 and 1966 the number of Russian-language schools in Ukrainian SSR increased, while the number of Ukrianian-language schools fell.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013203-205_(volume_3)-171)

In Ukraine, the removal of Khrushchev resulted in the political rise of [Petro Shelest](/wiki/Petro_Shelest "Petro Shelest"), who was considered to be a [Ukrainophile](/wiki/Ukrainophile "Ukrainophile") and worked to undo the Russificatory policies of his predecessor, promoting Ukrainian-language education, media and publishing. A loyal Communist by ideology, Shelest refused to recognize the elimination of national distinctions as a goal of Soviet Union's development, and promoted the notions of Soviet republics' sovereignty.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013239-241_(volume_3)-172) Shelest's removal from the post of the [First Secretary of the Communist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/First_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_Ukraine "First Secretary of the Communist Party of Ukraine") in 1972 signified a decisive turn to policies of Russification, which were implemented by his successor [Volodymyr Shcherbytsky](/wiki/Volodymyr_Shcherbytsky "Volodymyr Shcherbytsky"). The fight against supposed "Ukrainian nationalism" frequently adopted the forms of hysterical accusations against Ukrainians following their national traditions, and Soviet officials would demonstratively use Russian during official events. Even the statue of [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune") in Lviv's [Rynok Square](/wiki/Market_Square_(Lviv) "Market Square (Lviv)") had its [trident](/wiki/Trident "Trident") removed in order not to produce [unneeded associations](/wiki/Tryzub "Tryzub").[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013243_(volume_3)-173)

By the mid-1980s, only every fifth student in Kyiv had Ukrainian as language of school education. Russian language dominated in pedagogical schools and in higher education. A significant affair in Ukrianian culture during the Brezhnev era was the campaign against [Oles Honchar](/wiki/Oles_Honchar "Oles Honchar"), whose 1968 novel *[The Cathedral](/wiki/The_Cathedral_(Honchar_novel) "The Cathedral (Honchar novel)")* was attacked for criticizing local Communist authorities' attitudes to cultural heritage. As a result, in 1971 Honchar was removed from his position as head of the Writers' Union of Ukraine. Other authors residing in Ukraine, among them Oles Berdnyk, Ivan Dziuba, [Mykola Lukash](/wiki/Mykola_Lukash "Mykola Lukash"), [Lina Kostenko](/wiki/Lina_Kostenko "Lina Kostenko"), [Boris Chichibabin](/wiki/Boris_Chichibabin "Boris Chichibabin") and [Viktor Nekrasov](/wiki/Viktor_Nekrasov "Viktor Nekrasov") also became victims of government persecution. The crackdown against Ukrainian literature stopped in 1979, after [Pavlo Zahrebelnyi](/wiki/Pavlo_Zahrebelnyi "Pavlo Zahrebelnyi") was appointed to lead the Writers' Union.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013275-278_(volume_3)-174)

A highlight of the Brezhnev period was the emergence of [Ukrainian poetic cinema](/wiki/Ukrainian_poetic_cinema "Ukrainian poetic cinema") represented by works of Sergei Parajanov, [Volodymyr Denysenko](/wiki/Volodymyr_Denysenko "Volodymyr Denysenko"), [Ivan Mykolaichuk](/wiki/Ivan_Mykolaichuk "Ivan Mykolaichuk") and [Yuri Illenko](/wiki/Yuri_Ilyenko "Yuri Ilyenko"). However, just like in case of literature, [cinema of Ukraine](/wiki/Cinema_of_Ukraine "Cinema of Ukraine") during that time suffered from severe government pressure, which hindered its development. By the end of 1970s, the majority of films produced at [Dovzhenko Film Studios](/wiki/Dovzhenko_Film_Studios "Dovzhenko Film Studios") in Kyiv were filmed in Russian, and [Odessa Film Studios](/wiki/Odessa_Film_Studios "Odessa Film Studios") produced exclusively Russian-language movies. Foreign movies were [dubbed](/wiki/Dubbing "Dubbing") exclusively in Russian. The early 1970s also saw a crackdown against the revival of [Ukrainian folk songs](/wiki/Ukrainian_folk_music "Ukrainian folk music"), whose performers were blamed of "bourgeois nationalism". A significant cultural event of the Brezhnev era was the celebration of Kyiv's 1500th anniversary in 1982, which included the unveiling of several architectural monuments still defining the city's image: [Ukrainian House](/wiki/Ukrainian_House "Ukrainian House") (originally Lenin Museum), Hero City Obelisk in modern-day [Halytska Square](/wiki/Halytska_Square "Halytska Square"), [Monument to the Founders of Kyiv](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Founders_of_Kyiv "Monument to the Founders of Kyiv") and [Arch of Freedom of the Ukrainian People](/wiki/Arch_of_Freedom_of_the_Ukrainian_People "Arch of Freedom of the Ukrainian People") (Arch of People's Friendship).[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013278-282_(volume_3)-175)

### Final years of Soviet rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Final years of Soviet rule")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%B8_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%B4%D1%83%D1%85%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%86%D1%82%D0%B2%D0%B0._1990_%D1%80..JPG/250px-%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%B8_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%B4%D1%83%D1%85%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%86%D1%82%D0%B2%D0%B0._1990_%D1%80..JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%B8_%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%B4%D1%83%D1%85%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%86%D1%82%D0%B2%D0%B0._1990_%D1%80..JPG)

Members of Ukrainian clergy, including Greek Catholic priest [Yaroslav Lesiv](/wiki/Yaroslav_Lesiv "Yaroslav Lesiv") (in the middle) in 1990

The advent of Perestroika led to a spike in the Ukrainian national liberation movement, which was vividly demonstrated in the sphere of religion. Starting from 1987 numerous petitions were directed to authorities with demands to provide official recognition of the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church following more than 40 years of persecution. To neuter these demands, in April 1988 Gorbachev ordered [Patriarch Pimen I of Moscow](/wiki/Patriarch_Pimen_I_of_Moscow "Patriarch Pimen I of Moscow") to increase the number of Orthodox parishes in Western Ukraine and promote church publications in Ukrainian language. However, those measures had little effect on the Greek Catholic community. In May 1989 six bishops of the banned church organized a [hunger strike](/wiki/Hunger_strike "Hunger strike") in central Moscow, and in Western Ukraine a major action in support of religious freedom was organized on the initiative of [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)") [Myroslav Ivan Lubachivsky](/wiki/Myroslav_Ivan_Lubachivsky "Myroslav Ivan Lubachivsky"). On 17 September 1989 calls for legalization of the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church were issued by participants of a demonstration in memory of the 50th anniversary of the Soviet invasion, which was organized in Lviv. Answering to numerous [telegrams](/wiki/Telegram "Telegram") he received from Ukraine, on 5 October 1989 [pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") [John Paul II](/wiki/John_Paul_II "John Paul II") declared the official recognition of Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church by Soviet authorities to be a prerequisite for democratization in the USSR. On 23 January 1990 a council of Greek Catholic bishops in Lviv recognized the decision of the 1946 synod to be invalid and demanded full rehabilitation of the church and return of all of its properties. The victory of local Democratic Bloc representatives in elections to the Supreme Soviet of Ukraine made it possible to realize the council's demands, and Lviv's [St. George's Cathedral](/wiki/St._George%27s_Cathedral,_Lviv "St. George's Cathedral, Lviv") returned to Greek Catholics. In March 1991 cardinal Lubachivsky returned from emigration.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013328-331_(volume_3)-176)

Following the steps of Greek Catholics, in August 1989 the community of Sts. Peter and Paul Church in Lviv declared their refusal to subordinate itself to the Russian Orthodox Church and proclaimed its [autocephaly](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly"). On 20 October a congress of clergymen declared the restoration of the Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church, and in June 1990 an all-Ukrainian synod elected [Mstyslav Skrypnyk](/wiki/Mstyslav_Skrypnyk "Mstyslav Skrypnyk") as its patriarch. In January 1990 a synod of the Russian Orthodox Church in Moscow approved the transformation of its parishes in Ukraine into a self-governed [exarchate](/wiki/Exarchate "Exarchate") under the name of [Ukrainian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Orthodox_Church_-_Moscow_Patriarchate "Ukrainian Orthodox Church - Moscow Patriarchate") (UOC). By early 1991, 10,810 religious communities were registered in Ukraine, with the biggest confessions being represented by UOC (5031 communities), UGCC (2001), [Union of Evangelical Christians-Baptists](/wiki/All-Ukrainian_Union_of_Churches_of_Evangelical_Christian_Baptists "All-Ukrainian Union of Churches of Evangelical Christian Baptists") (1127) and UAOC (939).[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013331-332_(volume_3)-177)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Mykolayiv%2C_Namyv.jpg/250px-Mykolayiv%2C_Namyv.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mykolayiv,_Namyv.jpg)

[Microdistricts](/wiki/Microdistrict "Microdistrict"), such as this one in [Mykolaiv](/wiki/Mykolaiv "Mykolaiv"), became common sights throughout the Ukrainian SSR's cities.

Between 1959 and 1989 Soviet Ukraine's population increased by 9,6 million, reaching the number of 51,7 million people. This made Ukraine Europe's sixth most populated country after Russia, Germany, Italy, Great Britain and France. However, the population growth gradually slowed down, and was to a large degree contributed to by migration from other parts of the USSR. Decreasing [birth rates](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") and increasing [mortality](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") were combined with an increase in average [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy"), which led to the aging of population. Starting from late 1960s, rural areas of Ukraine started experiencing [depopulation](/wiki/Depopulation "Depopulation"). The ethnic composition of Ukrainian SSR changed with time, with the percentage of ethnic Ukrainians decreasing amid simultaneous increase in the number of ethnic Russians. Immigration played a significant role in this process. This contributed to Russification, especially in eastern and southern parts of the country. Nevertheless, even Ukrainians who adopted Russian as main language tended to preserve their Ukrainian identity.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013261-265_(volume_3)-178)

Urbanisation in post-Stalin Ukraine grew quickly; in 1959, only 25 cities in Ukraine had populations over one hundred thousand, by 1979 the number had grown to 49. During the same period, the growth of cities with a population over one million increased from one to five; Kiev alone nearly doubled its population, from 1.1 million in 1959 to 2.1 million in 1979. This proved a turning point in Ukrainian society: for the first time in Ukraine's history, the majority of ethnic Ukrainians lived in urban areas; 53 percent of the ethnic Ukrainian population did so in 1979. The majority worked in the non-agricultural sector, in 1970 31 percent of Ukrainians engaged in agriculture, in contrast, 63 percent of Ukrainians were industrial workers and white-collar staff. In 1959, 37 percent of Ukrainians lived in urban areas, in 1989 the proportion had increased to 60 percent.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1996713-179) During the same period, the share of rural inhabitants decreased from half of the population to one-third.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEКульчицький2013267_(volume_3)-152)
